abstract: Camera control has been actively studied in text or image conditioned video generation tasks. However, altering camera trajectories of a given video remains under-explored, despite its importance in the field of video creation. It is non-trivial due to the extra constraints of maintaining multiple-frame appearance and dynamic synchronization. To address this, we present ReCamMaster, a camera-controlled generative video re-rendering framework that reproduces the dynamic scene of an input video at novel camera trajectories. The core innovation lies in harnessing the generative capabilities of pre-trained text-to-video models through a simple yet powerful video conditioning mechanism--its capability is often overlooked in current research. To overcome the scarcity of qualified training data, we construct a comprehensive multi-camera synchronized video dataset using Unreal Engine 5, which is carefully curated to follow real-world filming characteristics, covering diverse scenes and camera movements. It helps the model generalize to in-the-wild videos. Lastly, we further improve the robustness to diverse inputs through a meticulously designed training strategy. Extensive experiments show that our method substantially outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches. Our method also finds promising applications in video stabilization, super-resolution, and outpainting. Our code and dataset are publicly available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: Concatenating source and target video tokens along the frame dimension allows the 3D self-attention mechanism of diffusion transformers to effectively reason across both videos, leading to superior spatio-temporal consistency and content synchronization.
Carefully designed large-scale synthetic datasets, combined with domain gap mitigation techniques, can effectively train models that generalize well to complex "in-the-wild" real-world videos.

Pre-trained text-to-video models possess substantial capabilities for sophisticated video-to-video conditional generation tasks when equipped with effective conditioning mechanisms and training strategies.
originalProblem: Professional-level camera movements are often difficult for amateur videographers due to expensive hardware and specialized skills, limiting creative flexibility in post-production.

Existing camera-controlled video re-rendering methods struggle with real-world "in-the-wild" videos due to domain gaps from synthetic training data, computational demands of per-video optimization, or reliance on inaccurate 4D reconstruction from single videos.
A scarcity of suitable multi-camera synchronized video datasets hinders the development of robust models for camera-controlled video generation.
results: Achieved superior visual quality and consistency with lower FID (57.10) and FVD (122.74) on standard benchmarks compared to baselines like DaS (FID 63.25, FVD 159.60) and GCD (FID 72.83, FVD 367.32).
Demonstrated better camera accuracy with lower rotation error (1.22) and translation error (4.85), alongside higher view synchronization indicated by more matching pixels (906.03K).

Qualitatively produced highly coherent video re-renderings with plausible out-of-view content generation, strong temporal synchronization, and robustness across diverse camera trajectories, significantly reducing artifacts seen in prior methods.
solution: ReCamMaster developed a framework built on a pre-trained text-to-video diffusion model, utilizing a novel "Frame Dimension Conditioning" to inject source video information by concatenating tokens along the frame dimension.
A learnable camera encoder is used to condition the model on target camera extrinsics (rotation and translation) for precise camera trajectory control, inferring the source camera path implicitly.
A large-scale, high-quality synthetic multi-camera synchronized dataset comprising 136K videos from 13.6K dynamic scenes was created using Unreal Engine 5 and publicly released.
paper_summary.summary: ReCamMaster introduces a framework for camera-controlled generative video re-rendering, enabling the synthesis of new videos from a single input video with novel camera trajectories. The method leverages a novel "Frame Dimension Conditioning" mechanism and a large-scale synthetic dataset, achieving improved visual quality, camera accuracy, and view synchronization over prior approaches.

title: ReCamMaster: Camera-Controlled Generative Rendering from A Single Video

abstract: Camera and human motion controls have been extensively studied for video generation, but existing approaches typically address them separately, suffering from limited data with high-quality annotations for both aspects. To overcome this, we present Uni3C, a unified 3D-enhanced framework for precise control of both camera and human motion in video generation. Uni3C includes two key contributions. First, we propose a plug-and-play control module trained with a frozen video generative backbone, PCDController, which utilizes unprojected point clouds from monocular depth to achieve accurate camera control. By leveraging the strong 3D priors of point clouds and the powerful capacities of video foundational models, PCDController shows impressive generalization, performing well regardless of whether the inference backbone is frozen or fine-tuned. This flexibility enables different modules of Uni3C to be trained in specific domains, i.e., either camera control or human motion control, reducing the dependency on jointly annotated data. Second, we propose a jointly aligned 3D world guidance for the inference phase that seamlessly integrates both scenic point clouds and SMPL-X characters to unify the control signals for camera and human motion, respectively. Extensive experiments confirm that PCDController enjoys strong robustness in driving camera motion for fine-tuned backbones of video generation. Uni3C substantially outperforms competitors in both camera controllability and human motion quality. Additionally, we collect tailored validation sets featuring challenging camera movements and human actions to validate the effectiveness of our method.

keyInsights: A modular, lightweight control module can effectively imbue large, frozen video diffusion models with precise camera control without sacrificing their generalization capabilities.
Explicitly aligning all 3D control signals (camera and human) into a unified 3D world space is crucial for generating physically plausible and visually consistent videos.
Strong 3D priors, particularly point clouds, are more effective for precise camera trajectory control and faster convergence than Plücker rays alone, especially when combined.
originalProblem: Existing video generation models lack precise, unified control over both camera trajectories and human motions.
Many control methods heavily modify foundational video diffusion models, impairing generalization and requiring costly, extensive joint training data.
Disparate 3D control signals (e.g., scenic geometry and human poses) often lead to incoherent guidance and physically unrealistic video outputs.
results: PCDController achieves superior camera control precision, demonstrating significantly lower Absolute Translation Error (ATE), Relative Translation Error (RPE), and Relative Rotation Error (RRE) compared to existing methods.
Uni3C substantially outperforms competitors in unified camera-human motion control, yielding higher visual quality and consistency as validated by VBench++ scores and user studies.

The framework enables flexible human motion transfer from diverse sources, supports detailed hand control via Hamer models, and maintains robust performance even with imperfect depth-derived point clouds.
solution: A lightweight, plug-and-play camera control module (PCDController) is introduced, trained with a frozen foundational VDM backbone using 3D geometric priors like warped point clouds and Plücker ray embeddings.
A Global 3D World Guidance mechanism aligns diverse 3D conditions—scenic point clouds, SMPL-X human models, and Hamer hand models—into a consistent 3D space during inference.
The framework integrates with advanced human animation backbones (e.g., RealisDance-DiT) by externally adding PCDController features, enabling seamless, unified control without joint fine-tuning.
paper_summary.summary: The Uni3C framework from Alibaba DAMO Academy and Fudan University unifies precise 3D-enhanced camera and human motion controls for video generation, utilizing a lightweight, plug-and-play module and global 3D world guidance to achieve superior control accuracy and video quality compared to existing methods. This approach reduces hallucination rates while supporting flexible motion transfer and preserving foundational model generalization.

title: Uni3C: Unifying Precisely 3D-Enhanced Camera and Human Motion Controls for Video Generation

abstract:
We present TrajectoryCrafter, a novel approach to redirect camera
trajectories for monocular videos. By disentangling deterministic view
transformations from stochastic content generation, our method achieves precise
control over user-specified camera trajectories. We propose a novel dual-stream
conditional video diffusion model that concurrently integrates point cloud
renders and source videos as conditions, ensuring accurate view transformations
and coherent 4D content generation. Instead of leveraging scarce multi-view
videos, we curate a hybrid training dataset combining web-scale monocular
videos with static multi-view datasets, by our innovative double-reprojection
strategy, significantly fostering robust generalization across diverse scenes.
Extensive evaluations on multi-view and large-scale monocular videos
demonstrate the superior performance of our method.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Explicitly disentangling deterministic view transformations from stochastic content generation improves control
Double-reprojection strategy enables effective training data generation from monocular videos
Two-stage training scheme helps balance geometric accuracy with detail preservation
originalProblem: Existing methods for novel view synthesis either require multi-view data or struggle with temporal consistency
Camera trajectory control in monocular videos is challenging due to limited 3D information and dynamic content
results: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on both multi-view and large-scale monocular video datasets
Generates high-fidelity videos with accurate camera trajectories and consistent content
Superior performance across metrics including PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS, and VBench compared to baselines
Successfully handles diverse real-world scenes while maintaining temporal coherence
solution: Dual-stream conditional video diffusion model combining point cloud renders and source video
Novel Reference-conditioned Diffusion Transformer (Ref-DiT) block for improved fidelity
Hybrid training approach using both web-scale monocular videos and static multi-view datasets
paper_summary.summary: Tencent and CUHK researchers introduce TrajectoryCrafter, a groundbreaking framework that enables high-quality camera trajectory redirection in monocular videos through an innovative dual-stream diffusion model, achieving state-of-the-art performance in novel view synthesis while maintaining temporal consistency through clever separation of view transformation and content generation.

title: TrajectoryCrafter: Redirecting Camera Trajectory for Monocular Videos  via Diffusion Models

abstract:
The development of Text-to-Video (T2V) generation has made motion transfer
possible, enabling the control of video motion based on existing footage.
However, current methods have two limitations: 1) struggle to handle
multi-subjects videos, failing to transfer specific subject motion; 2) struggle
to preserve the diversity and accuracy of motion as transferring to subjects
with varying shapes. To overcome these, we introduce \textbf{ConMo}, a
zero-shot framework that disentangle and recompose the motions of subjects and
camera movements. ConMo isolates individual subject and background motion cues
from complex trajectories in source videos using only subject masks, and
reassembles them for target video generation. This approach enables more
accurate motion control across diverse subjects and improves performance in
multi-subject scenarios. Additionally, we propose soft guidance in the
recomposition stage which controls the retention of original motion to adjust
shape constraints, aiding subject shape adaptation and semantic transformation.
Unlike previous methods, ConMo unlocks a wide range of applications, including
subject size and position editing, subject removal, semantic modifications, and
camera motion simulation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ConMo
significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in motion fidelity and
semantic consistency. The code is available at
this https URL

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

keyInsights: Separating compound motions into individual subject and background components enables better control
Using Local Spatial Marginal Means (LSMM) helps isolate motion cues for overlapping subjects
Blending subject motion with camera trajectory control weights reduces semantic constraints
originalProblem: Existing motion transfer methods struggle with multiple subjects and complex motion patterns
Current approaches have difficulty preserving motion fidelity when transferring to subjects with different shapes and semantics
results: Achieves better text alignment and motion fidelity compared to baseline methods
Successfully handles multiple subject scenarios and shape adaptations
Enables new applications like subject size/position editing and camera motion simulation
Shows some limitations with incomplete mask segmentation
solution: Motion disentanglement stage separates individual subject motions using subject masks and local feature differences
Soft guidance strategy enables flexible motion recomposition during video generation while maintaining motion consistency
Integration with Video Diffusion Models for spatiotemporal coherence
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Peking University and BUPT introduce ConMo, a framework for zero-shot motion transfer in text-to-video generation that disentangles and recomposes individual subject motions from reference videos, enabling precise control over multiple subjects while preserving motion accuracy across different shapes and semantics.

title: ConMo: Controllable Motion Disentanglement and Recomposition for  Zero-Shot Motion Transfer

abstract: Camera-controlled generative video re-rendering methods, such as ReCamMaster, have achieved remarkable progress. However, despite their success in single-view setting, these works often struggle to maintain consistency across multi-view scenarios. Ensuring spatio-temporal coherence in hallucinated regions remains challenging due to the inherent stochasticity of generative models. To address it, we introduce PlenopticDreamer, a framework that synchronizes generative hallucinations to maintain spatio-temporal memory. The core idea is to train a multi-in-single-out video-conditioned model in an autoregressive manner, aided by a camera-guided video retrieval strategy that adaptively selects salient videos from previous generations as conditional inputs. In addition, Our training incorporates progressive context-scaling to improve convergence, self-conditioning to enhance robustness against long-range visual degradation caused by error accumulation, and a long-video conditioning mechanism to support extended video generation. Extensive experiments on the Basic and Agibot benchmarks demonstrate that PlenopticDreamer achieves state-of-the-art video re-rendering, delivering superior view synchronization, high-fidelity visuals, accurate camera control, and diverse view transformations (e.g., third-person to third-person, and head-view to gripper-view in robotic manipulation). Project page: this https URL

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Integrating a memory-based, autoregressive approach is crucial for achieving long-term spatio-temporal coherence and consistent scene generation across diverse camera viewpoints.
Intelligent 3D FOV-based video retrieval, which considers spatial co-visibility, significantly enhances view synchronization compared to arbitrary context selection.

Progressive context-scaling and self-conditioned training are effective for stabilizing the optimization of large generative models and improving robustness against error propagation in recursive generation.
originalProblem: Existing camera-controlled video re-rendering methods struggle with spatio-temporal consistency across multi-view scenarios, leading to geometric misalignment and visual desynchronization in generated videos.
Prior generative models for video often fail to maintain long-range spatial memory, particularly when hallucinating previously unseen regions, limiting their utility for holistic scene understanding.
Producing coherent plenoptic functions (consistent light fields) across space and time, especially for complex camera trajectories and long video sequences, remains a significant challenge.
results: PlenopticDreamer achieves state-of-the-art view synchronization, with 41.2K Matched Pixels (vs. 36.2K for TrajectoryCrafter) and the lowest FVD of 425.8 (vs. 675.4 for ReCamMaster*) on the Basic benchmark.

The framework maintains competitive camera accuracy (TransErr of 0.54, RotErr of 0.21 rad) while successfully generating videos from diverse camera transformations, including challenging head-view to gripper-view shifts for embodied AI.

The long-video conditioning mechanism enables coherent generation of extended video sequences, and ablation studies confirm the critical role of progressive context-scaling, self-conditioned training, and 3D FOV–based retrieval in performance.
solution: An autoregressive, multi-in–single-out generation paradigm is employed, where each target video is conditioned on a memory bank of previously generated video-camera pairs, leveraging a flow-based Video Diffusion Transformer (DiT).
A 3D Field-of-View (FOV)–based video retrieval mechanism intelligently selects the most spatially co-visible past video segments from memory to ensure multi-view consistency.

Novel training strategies, including progressive context-scaling and self-conditioned training, are introduced to stabilize optimization and mitigate error accumulation for robust and scalable generation.
paper_summary.summary: PlenopticDreamer, developed by NVIDIA, CUHK, and Georgia Tech, introduces an autoregressive, memory-based framework for camera-controlled video re-rendering that enforces spatio-temporal consistency across multiple views. It achieves superior view synchronization with the highest Matched Pixels (41.2K) and lowest FVD (425.8) on the Basic benchmark, while also supporting diverse camera transformations and coherent long-video generation.

title: Plenoptic Video Generation

abstract: Recent video generation models demonstrate impressive synthesis capabilities but remain limited by single-modality conditioning, constraining their holistic world understanding. This stems from insufficient cross-modal interaction and limited modal diversity for comprehensive world knowledge representation. To address these limitations, we introduce UnityVideo, a unified framework for world-aware video generation that jointly learns across multiple modalities (segmentation masks, human skeletons, DensePose, optical flow, and depth maps) and training paradigms. Our approach features two core components: (1) dynamic noising to unify heterogeneous training paradigms, and (2) a modality switcher with an in-context learner that enables unified processing via modular parameters and contextual learning. We contribute a large-scale unified dataset with 1.3M samples. Through joint optimization, UnityVideo accelerates convergence and significantly enhances zero-shot generalization to unseen data. We demonstrate that UnityVideo achieves superior video quality, consistency, and improved alignment with physical world constraints. Code and data can be found at: this https URL

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Unifying diverse visual sub-modalities and video-related tasks within a single framework significantly enhances 'world awareness', leading to generated videos that are more physically consistent and realistic.
Joint training across multiple modalities and tasks leads to accelerated convergence and mutual performance gains, demonstrating effective knowledge transfer and synergy.

The In-Context Learner and Modality-Adaptive Switcher designs facilitate strong zero-shot generalization capabilities, allowing the model to interpret and produce novel combinations of modalities and generalize to unseen visual concepts.
originalProblem: Current video models predominantly rely on single-modality conditions (e.g., text-to-video) or estimate only one auxiliary modality, limiting their holistic understanding of the 'world' depicted in videos.
A fragmented approach to training, where different tasks (generation, estimation) or modalities are handled separately, prevents cross-task synergy and slows down convergence.
Existing models often struggle with physical consistency and robust zero-shot generalization to unseen objects or styles, indicating a lack of deeper 'world awareness'.
results: UnityVideo achieves competitive or superior performance across text-to-video, controllable generation, and modality estimation benchmarks, outperforming state-of-the-art specialized models.

Qualitative results demonstrate enhanced physical reasoning (e.g., accurate refraction) and better adherence to control conditions, with superior video quality and consistency compared to previous methods.

Ablation studies confirm that both the unified multi-modal and multi-task training approaches, along with the In-Context Learner and Modality-Adaptive Switcher, contribute to overall performance improvements and robustness.
solution: A single diffusion transformer (DiT) backbone is employed to unify three training paradigms: conditional generation, modality estimation, and joint generation, optimized concurrently via dynamic task routing.

Multiple modalities are integrated using an In-Context Learner, which uses modality-specific textual prompts, and a Modality-Adaptive Switcher, which applies learnable modality-specific modulation parameters within each DiT block.
A two-stage curriculum learning strategy, along with the curated OpenUni dataset comprising 1.3 million multimodal video samples, enables robust training and generalization.
paper_summary.summary: UnityVideo establishes a unified multi-modal and multi-task learning framework for video generation, enhancing a model's understanding of the physical world by jointly training on diverse visual sub-modalities like depth, optical flow, and segmentation. The framework achieves superior quantitative and qualitative performance across text-to-video generation, controllable generation, and modality estimation tasks.

title: UnityVideo: Unified Multi-Modal Multi-Task Learning for Enhancing World-Aware Video Generation

abstract:
Camera control, which achieves diverse visual effects by changing camera
position and pose, has attracted widespread attention. However, existing
methods face challenges such as complex interaction and limited control
capabilities. To address these issues, we present OmniCam, a unified multimodal
camera control framework. Leveraging large language models and video diffusion
models, OmniCam generates spatio-temporally consistent videos. It supports
various combinations of input modalities: the user can provide text or video
with expected trajectory as camera path guidance, and image or video as content
reference, enabling precise control over camera motion. To facilitate the
training of OmniCam, we introduce the OmniTr dataset, which contains a large
collection of high-quality long-sequence trajectories, videos, and
corresponding descriptions. Experimental results demonstrate that our model
achieves state-of-the-art performance in high-quality camera-controlled video
generation across various metrics.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Decomposing camera control into discrete motion representations enables a unified pipeline for diverse input modalities.
Leveraging 3D reconstruction and diffusion models allows for generating consistent video content from novel camera perspectives.
End-to-end optimization, specifically using reinforcement learning, refines both trajectory generation and video synthesis for improved output quality.
originalProblem: Existing video generation methods lack intuitive and precise control over camera movements, limiting creative potential.
Current approaches struggle to maintain spatial structure and content coherence during complex camera motions.
There is a lack of comprehensive datasets for training and evaluating camera-controlled video generation models.
results: OmniCam achieves better perceptual similarity (lower LPIPS) and structural similarity (higher SSIM) compared to state-of-the-art methods like ViewCrafter and FSGS.
User studies indicate superior performance across multiple dimensions, particularly in guidance following and video motion quality.
The introduced OmniTr dataset contains over 10,000 camera trajectory sequences with corresponding text descriptions, facilitating training and evaluation for camera-controlled video generation.
solution: OmniCam employs a two-stage framework: first generating a precise camera trajectory from various inputs (text, video, or direct specification), then synthesizing video guided by this trajectory.
It utilizes a discrete motion representation as an intermediate step to convert user inputs (text parsed by an LLM, video trajectories extracted via SLAM) into a continuous camera path.

Video synthesis combines 3D scene reconstruction using DUSt3R, initial rendering along the trajectory, and diffusion-based refinement with end-to-end reinforcement learning optimization to ensure visual quality and spatial consistency.
paper_summary.summary: OmniCam introduces a unified framework for camera-controlled video generation that supports diverse input modalities like text and reference videos. The system generates videos with precise, frame-level camera movements while maintaining spatial consistency and achieving improved perceptual and structural similarity compared to prior methods.

title: OmniCam: Unified Multimodal Video Generation via Camera Control

abstract:
This paper introduces CameraCtrl II, a framework that enables large-scale
dynamic scene exploration through a camera-controlled video diffusion model.
Previous camera-conditioned video generative models suffer from diminished
video dynamics and limited range of viewpoints when generating videos with
large camera movement. We take an approach that progressively expands the
generation of dynamic scenes -- first enhancing dynamic content within
individual video clip, then extending this capability to create seamless
explorations across broad viewpoint ranges. Specifically, we construct a
dataset featuring a large degree of dynamics with camera parameter annotations
for training while designing a lightweight camera injection module and training
scheme to preserve dynamics of the pretrained models. Building on these
improved single-clip techniques, we enable extended scene exploration by
allowing users to iteratively specify camera trajectories for generating
coherent video sequences. Experiments across diverse scenarios demonstrate that
CameraCtrl Ii enables camera-controlled dynamic scene synthesis with
substantially wider spatial exploration than previous approaches.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Injecting camera parameters only at the initial layer of a diffusion model, combined with joint training on labeled and unlabeled data, helps preserve dynamic content while enabling precise camera control.

A specially curated dataset (REALCAMDataset) containing dynamic real-world videos with extracted and calibrated camera parameters is crucial for training models that generate dynamic camera-controlled scenes.
A clip-wise autoregressive generation strategy allows for the creation of extended, coherent video sequences, enabling broader spatial exploration of generated environments.
originalProblem: Existing camera-controlled video generation models often produce static scenes, leading to degraded video dynamics when camera control is applied.
Current methods are limited to generating short video clips, restricting the extent of spatial exploration within synthesized environments.
Available datasets for camera-controlled video generation primarily consist of static scenes, lacking the diversity and movement found in real-world dynamic content.
results: CameraCtrl II outperforms existing methods (MotionCtrl, CameraCtrl, AC3D) across metrics such as Fréchet Video Distance (FVD), Motion Strength, Camera Control Accuracy, and Geometric and Appearance Consistency.

Ablation studies confirmed the positive impact of each proposed component, including the REALCAMDataset's dynamic videos and calibration, the lightweight camera injection module, joint training, and clip-level video extension.
Qualitative results demonstrate the model's capacity to generate diverse and realistic scenes with accurate camera control and maintained dynamic elements over extended periods.
solution: Introduces REALCAMDataset, a new dynamic video dataset with meticulously extracted and calibrated camera parameter annotations from real-world footage.
Develops a lightweight camera injection module that integrates camera parameters into the diffusion model's initial layer, preventing over-constraining of pixel generation.

Implements a joint training strategy using both camera-labeled and unlabeled video data, enabling the model to retain pre-trained dynamic generation capabilities while learning camera control, and employs clip-wise autoregressive generation for extended scene exploration.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from The Chinese University of Hong Kong, ByteDance Seed, and Stanford University developed CameraCtrl II, a framework for large-scale dynamic scene exploration via camera-controlled video generation. The system addresses limitations in video dynamics and spatial exploration by introducing a new dynamic dataset (REALCAMDataset) and a lightweight camera injection module, yielding improved video quality and camera control accuracy.

title: CameraCtrl II: Dynamic Scene Exploration via Camera-controlled Video  Diffusion Models

abstract: Modern generative video models excel at producing convincing, high-quality outputs, but struggle to maintain multi-view and spatiotemporal consistency in highly dynamic real-world environments. In this work, we introduce \textbf{AnyView}, a diffusion-based video generation framework for \emph{dynamic view synthesis} with minimal inductive biases or geometric assumptions. We leverage multiple data sources with various levels of supervision, including monocular (2D), multi-view static (3D) and multi-view dynamic (4D) datasets, to train a generalist spatiotemporal implicit representation capable of producing zero-shot novel videos from arbitrary camera locations and trajectories. We evaluate AnyView on standard benchmarks, showing competitive results with the current state of the art, and propose \textbf{AnyViewBench}, a challenging new benchmark tailored towards \emph{extreme} dynamic view synthesis in diverse real-world scenarios. In this more dramatic setting, we find that most baselines drastically degrade in performance, as they require significant overlap between viewpoints, while AnyView maintains the ability to produce realistic, plausible, and spatiotemporally consistent videos when prompted from \emph{any} viewpoint. Results, data, code, and models can be viewed at: this https URL

keyInsights: An implicit, data-driven approach, learned from a vast and diverse set of 4D data, can effectively overcome the limitations of explicit geometric modeling for novel view synthesis in dynamic, extreme scenarios.
Encoding camera parameters using a unified Plücker representation within a latent diffusion transformer allows for precise 4D control and robust generation across complex camera trajectories.

Minimal inductive biases in the generative model, combined with comprehensive training data, enable the framework to infer complex scene geometry, physics, and object permanence without direct 3D reconstruction.
originalProblem: Existing Dynamic View Synthesis (DVS) methods often fail to generate realistic novel views in "extreme" scenarios characterized by large camera displacements and minimal overlap with input views, largely relying on limited extrapolation or copying.
Current DVS benchmarks primarily focus on "narrow" settings with small viewpoint changes, failing to evaluate models' robustness in real-world, highly dynamic and complex environments.

Explicit 3D reconstruction approaches, common in DVS, are computationally expensive, prone to compounding errors from depth estimation, and struggle with generalization to unobserved regions in dynamic scenes.
results: AnyView establishes state-of-the-art performance on the newly introduced AnyViewBench, a benchmark designed for "extreme" DVS, significantly outperforming existing baselines across metrics like PSNR, SSIM, and LPIPS.

The model achieves an average PSNR of 17.78 dB on in-distribution datasets of AnyViewBench, demonstrating its capability to handle drastic camera displacements and synthesize unobserved scene portions.

Qualitative results highlight the model's ability to generate perceptually realistic, spatiotemporally consistent videos, even inferring plausible physical interactions and object trajectories from limited input.
solution: Utilizes a diffusion-based video generation framework, building upon a latent diffusion transformer (Cosmos) and foregoing explicit 3D reconstruction or depth warping to avoid compounding errors.
Incorporates camera conditioning through a unified Plücker representation for per-pixel ray and moment vectors, providing 4D awareness and control over viewpoint changes.
Trains on an extensive and diverse mixture of 12 different 4D datasets spanning robotics, driving, and static/dynamic 3D scenes to learn robust implicit multi-view geometry and appearance priors.
paper_summary.summary: AnyView, developed by researchers at Toyota Research Institute and Amazon Web Services, enables the synthesis of perceptually realistic 4D videos from arbitrary viewpoints within dynamic scenes. The framework demonstrates robust novel view generation under extreme camera displacements, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the new AnyViewBench benchmark and surpassing existing baselines without relying on explicit 3D reconstruction.

title: AnyView: Synthesizing Any Novel View in Dynamic Scenes

abstract:
Controllability plays a crucial role in video generation, as it allows users
to create and edit content more precisely. Existing models, however, lack
control of camera pose that serves as a cinematic language to express deeper
narrative nuances. To alleviate this issue, we introduce CameraCtrl, enabling
accurate camera pose control for video diffusion models. Our approach explores
effective camera trajectory parameterization along with a plug-and-play camera
pose control module that is trained on top of a video diffusion model, leaving
other modules of the base model untouched. Moreover, a comprehensive study on
the effect of various training datasets is conducted, suggesting that videos
with diverse camera distributions and similar appearance to the base model
indeed enhance controllability and generalization. Experimental results
demonstrate the effectiveness of CameraCtrl in achieving precise camera control
with different video generation models, marking a step forward in the pursuit
of dynamic and customized video storytelling from textual and camera pose
inputs.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Plücker embeddings offer a geometrically meaningful and uniform camera pose representation, proving more effective for precise camera control than traditional numerical parameters.

A plug-and-play camera encoder, which inputs only Plücker embeddings and incorporates temporal attention, is crucial for preventing appearance leakage from training data and ensuring broad generalization across diverse video styles.

Injecting camera features into the temporal attention layers of the U-Net within the diffusion model effectively captures global view changes induced by camera motion, leading to superior control compared to spatial injection points.
originalProblem: Existing video diffusion models offer limited or no precise control over camera viewpoints and trajectories, hindering cinematic expression and specific visual narratives.

Prior camera control methods struggle with generalizing to arbitrary user-defined trajectories and often rely on numerical camera parameters that lack sufficient geometric understanding, leading to imprecise control.
Integrating control mechanisms frequently requires fine-tuning large base video diffusion models, making them less adaptable to the rapidly evolving ecosystem of personalized video generators.
results: CameraCtrl consistently achieves superior camera control accuracy, outperforming methods like MotionCtrl and AnimateDiff (MotionLoRA) with lower Translation Error (TransErr) and Rotation Error (RotErr) on both T2V (AnimateDiff) and I2V (SVD) base models.
The method successfully distinguishes camera movement from object motion within the scene and accurately reproduces complex, subtle camera trajectories, a challenge for previous approaches.

CameraCtrl preserves the overall video quality and object dynamics, with comparable or better FVD, CLIPSIM, FC, and Object Dynamic Degree (ODD) metrics compared to base models, confirming seamless integration without quality degradation.
solution: Introduces CameraCtrl, a plug-and-play module that integrates with pre-trained video diffusion models, enabling flexible and precise control over camera viewpoints without modifying the base model's core architecture.
Utilizes Plücker embeddings, a 6-dimensional pixel-wise geometric representation, to describe camera pose information, providing a more effective and interpretable signal for the diffusion model.

Employs a dedicated camera encoder with temporal attention to process Plücker embedding sequences and fuses the extracted camera features into the temporal attention layers of the U-Net within the base video diffusion model.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, and Stanford University developed CameraCtrl, a plug-and-play module that provides precise camera control for video diffusion models. This method uses Plücker embeddings for camera representation and integrates into existing models to enable accurate, generalizable camera trajectories while preserving video quality, outperforming prior approaches like MotionCtrl and AnimateDiff.

title: CameraCtrl: Enabling Camera Control for Text-to-Video Generation

abstract: Recent advances in illumination control extend image-based methods to video, yet still facing a trade-off between lighting fidelity and temporal consistency. Moving beyond relighting, a key step toward generative modeling of real-world scenes is the joint control of camera trajectory and illumination, since visual dynamics are inherently shaped by both geometry and lighting. To this end, we present Light-X, a video generation framework that enables controllable rendering from monocular videos with both viewpoint and illumination control. 1) We propose a disentangled design that decouples geometry and lighting signals: geometry and motion are captured via dynamic point clouds projected along user-defined camera trajectories, while illumination cues are provided by a relit frame consistently projected into the same geometry. These explicit, fine-grained cues enable effective disentanglement and guide high-quality illumination. 2) To address the lack of paired multi-view and multi-illumination videos, we introduce Light-Syn, a degradation-based pipeline with inverse-mapping that synthesizes training pairs from in-the-wild monocular footage. This strategy yields a dataset covering static, dynamic, and AI-generated scenes, ensuring robust training. Extensive experiments show that Light-X outperforms baseline methods in joint camera-illumination control and surpasses prior video relighting methods under both text- and background-conditioned settings.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Explicitly decoupling geometric/motion cues from illumination cues and providing them as distinct conditioning signals to a diffusion model allows for robust and flexible joint manipulation of camera and lighting.

Combining fine-grained, per-frame geometric and lighting information with a global illumination token within a Diffusion Transformer architecture is crucial for maintaining both local visual fidelity and temporal consistency of lighting across generated video frames.

Synthetic training data generated via an 'inverse mapping' degradation strategy effectively addresses the scarcity of real-world multi-view and multi-illumination datasets, enabling the training of complex generative models for high-dimensional control tasks.
originalProblem: Prior research in video generation independently addressed either video relighting (lacking camera control) or camera-controlled synthesis (lacking illumination control), leaving their joint manipulation as an open challenge.
Real-world videos capture complex scenes with intertwined geometry, motion, and illumination, making disentangled and coherent control difficult from limited monocular input.

Training models for joint camera-illumination control requires extensive paired multi-view and multi-illumination video datasets, which are exceptionally challenging to acquire in real-world settings.
results: Light-X achieved an FID score of 101.06 for joint camera-illumination control, outperforming baselines (e.g., 122.73 for the best baseline combination), demonstrating superior relighting fidelity and motion preservation.

For video relighting tasks, Light-X recorded an FID of 83.65 and an Aesthetic score of 0.645 for text-conditioned relighting, showing both high lighting accuracy and temporal coherence compared to other methods.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical contributions of all three diverse training data sources (static, dynamic, AI-generated), the fine-grained and global lighting control components, and the degradation-based data curation strategy to the model's overall performance and robustness.
solution: A disentangled conditioning scheme that explicitly separates camera control (using dynamic point clouds for geometry/motion) and illumination control (using a single relit frame projected onto scene geometry).

A Diffusion Transformer-based video diffusion model (Light-DiT) that integrates fine-grained vision tokens (from projected source views and relit projections) and global illumination tokens (from the initial relit frame) for consistent control.

Light-Syn, a degradation-based data curation pipeline that synthesizes diverse paired multi-view and multi-illumination training data by inversely mapping ground truth geometry and illumination onto degraded in-the-wild videos.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from S-Lab at NTU, BAAI, and other institutions developed LIGHT-X, a framework for generative 4D video rendering that provides joint control over camera trajectory and scene illumination from a single monocular video. The model achieves superior perceptual quality and temporal consistency in generated videos, outperforming existing methods in both joint control and video relighting tasks.

title: Light-X: Generative 4D Video Rendering with Camera and Illumination Control

abstract:
Numerous works have recently integrated 3D camera control into foundational
text-to-video models, but the resulting camera control is often imprecise, and
video generation quality suffers. In this work, we analyze camera motion from a
first principles perspective, uncovering insights that enable precise 3D camera
manipulation without compromising synthesis quality. First, we determine that
motion induced by camera movements in videos is low-frequency in nature. This
motivates us to adjust train and test pose conditioning schedules, accelerating
training convergence while improving visual and motion quality. Then, by
probing the representations of an unconditional video diffusion transformer, we
observe that they implicitly perform camera pose estimation under the hood, and
only a sub-portion of their layers contain the camera information. This
suggested us to limit the injection of camera conditioning to a subset of the
architecture to prevent interference with other video features, leading to a 4x
reduction of training parameters, improved training speed, and 10% higher
visual quality. Finally, we complement the typical dataset for camera control
learning with a curated dataset of 20K diverse, dynamic videos with stationary
cameras. This helps the model distinguish between camera and scene motion and
improves the dynamics of generated pose-conditioned videos. We compound these
findings to design the Advanced 3D Camera Control (AC3D) architecture, the new
state-of-the-art model for generative video modeling with camera control.

keyInsights: Camera motion is a low-frequency signal primarily determined in the early stages of the VDiT denoising process.
VDiTs implicitly learn camera pose, with the most accurate and disentangled representations peaking in their middle layers (blocks #13-21).
Training on static-biased datasets like RealEstate10k inadvertently teaches models to suppress scene dynamics when camera control is active.
originalProblem: Existing methods for 3D camera control in text-to-video (T2V) models often yield imprecise camera movements.
Integrating explicit camera control can degrade overall video quality and reduce scene dynamism.
A lack of fundamental understanding of how Video Diffusion Transformers (VDiTs) internally process camera motion leads to suboptimal control mechanisms.
Biases in existing camera pose datasets, such as an overrepresentation of static scenes, cause models to suppress inherent scene dynamics.
results: AC3D demonstrated 18% higher video fidelity and 25% more precise camera steering compared to its closest competitor, VD3D+DiT.
User studies showed a strong preference for AC3D, with participants favoring its overall quality in 95% of cases against VD3D (FIT) and 92.5% against VD3D (DiT).

Ablation studies validated the effectiveness of proposed components, with insights leading to a 4x reduction in trainable parameters and 15% faster training while improving visual quality and camera following.
solution: Analyzed the spectral properties of camera motion, leading to a strategy of restricting camera conditioning to early, low-frequency denoising steps.
Performed linear probing on VDiTs to identify where camera pose knowledge resides, limiting conditioning injection to the first 8 DiT blocks.
Curated a new dataset of 20K diverse, dynamic videos with stationary cameras to mitigate training data bias towards static scenes.
Implemented architectural and training improvements including separate text/camera guidance and enhanced ControlNet-like conditioning with feedback.
paper_summary.summary: AC3D introduces a method for analyzing and improving 3D camera control in pre-trained Video Diffusion Transformers, achieving 18% higher video fidelity and 25% more precise camera steering compared to prior methods. The approach leverages insights into the model's internal processing of motion and refined training data to enhance control accuracy and video quality.

title: AC3D: Analyzing and Improving 3D Camera Control in Video Diffusion  Transformers

abstract: Modeling scenes using video generation models has garnered growing research interest in recent years. However, most existing approaches rely on perspective video models that synthesize only limited observations of a scene, leading to issues of completeness and global consistency. We propose OmniRoam, a controllable panoramic video generation framework that exploits the rich per-frame scene coverage and inherent long-term spatial and temporal consistency of panoramic representation, enabling long-horizon scene wandering. Our framework begins with a preview stage, where a trajectory-controlled video generation model creates a quick overview of the scene from a given input image or video. Then, in the refine stage, this video is temporally extended and spatially upsampled to produce long-range, high-resolution videos, thus enabling high-fidelity world wandering. To train our model, we introduce two panoramic video datasets that incorporate both synthetic and real-world captured videos. Experiments show that our framework consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of visual quality, controllability, and long-term scene consistency, both qualitatively and quantitatively. We further showcase several extensions of this framework, including real-time video generation and 3D reconstruction. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Panoramic representations intrinsically offer holistic scene coverage and maintain global spatial memory, proving crucial for robust long-term consistency in large-scale scene generation.

A global-to-local generation strategy effectively mitigates the accumulated errors and temporal drift typically observed in direct autoregressive generation, particularly for extended video sequences.
The explicit decomposition of camera motion into scale and flow components provides an intuitive and precise control mechanism for synthesizing specific camera trajectories in generated scenes.
originalProblem: Perspective video models struggle with complete scene representation and global consistency due to their limited field-of-view, hindering comprehensive environmental modeling.
Existing long-horizon video generation methods often suffer from accumulated errors and temporal drift, especially during extensive camera movements, degrading quality over time.
Prior panoramic generation approaches frequently adapt perspective architectures without fully leveraging panoramic representations' inherent advantages for long-term spatial and temporal coherence.
results: OmniRoam quantitatively outperforms baseline methods in visual quality (e.g., FAED of 5.27 compared to Matrix-3D's 8.64 for 81-frame 480p videos) and trajectory controllability (e.g., PSNR'75 of 19.87 versus Matrix-3D's 17.04).

Achieves superior long-term spatial coherence, demonstrated by a higher `C_loop` score (e.g., 2.34 compared to Matrix-3D's 1.38 for 81-frame 480p videos), indicating better visual consistency upon loop closure.

The framework enables practical extensions, including a real-time preview mode generating 81-frame panoramic videos in approximately 7 seconds, and successfully reconstructs coherent 3D Gaussian Splatting scenes from its generated videos.
solution: A two-stage camera-controlled framework (preview-refine) for panoramic video synthesis is developed, starting with coarse panoramic video generation and followed by segment-wise refinement for temporal and spatial enhancement.
A canonical panoramic coordinate system is established to define trajectories strictly via translation, eliminating camera self-rotation for simplified, robust generation.
Camera motion control is decomposed into orthogonal scale (playback speed) and flow (directional displacement) components for fine-grained and intuitive trajectory conditioning.
paper_summary.summary: OmniRoam introduces a two-stage global-to-local framework for generating long-horizon panoramic videos that enable "world wandering" through synthesized environments. The method achieves improved visual quality, more precise trajectory control, and enhanced long-term scene consistency on established benchmarks compared to prior methods.

title: OmniRoam: World Wandering via Long-Horizon Panoramic Video Generation

abstract: Recent advancements in video diffusion models have shown exceptional abilities in simulating real-world dynamics and maintaining 3D consistency. This progress inspires us to investigate the potential of these models to ensure dynamic consistency across various viewpoints, a highly desirable feature for applications such as virtual filming. Unlike existing methods focused on multi-view generation of single objects for 4D reconstruction, our interest lies in generating open-world videos from arbitrary viewpoints, incorporating 6 DoF camera poses. To achieve this, we propose a plug-and-play module that enhances a pre-trained text-to-video model for multi-camera video generation, ensuring consistent content across different viewpoints. Specifically, we introduce a multi-view synchronization module to maintain appearance and geometry consistency across these viewpoints. Given the scarcity of high-quality training data, we design a hybrid training scheme that leverages multi-camera images and monocular videos to supplement Unreal Engine-rendered multi-camera videos. Furthermore, our method enables intriguing extensions, such as re-rendering a video from novel viewpoints. We also release a multi-view synchronized video dataset, named SynCamVideo-Dataset. Project page: this https URL.

keyInsights: The MVS module's cross-view self-attention effectively learns and maintains appearance, geometric, and dynamic consistency across multiple, diverse viewpoints.
A hybrid data training strategy, leveraging synthetic data for synchronization and real-world data for generalization and visual quality, effectively addresses the data scarcity problem.
Progressive training is essential for accurate camera pose following, especially when generating videos from widely divergent camera angles.
A learned spatial correspondence through full attention within the MVS module proves more effective for open-domain generation than explicit epipolar-constrained attention.
originalProblem: Existing controllable video generation methods primarily focus on single video sequences, not simultaneous multi-camera outputs.
Previous multi-view generation is largely confined to 4D object synthesis or fixed camera positions, lacking generalization to open-domain scenes with diverse viewpoints.
A scarcity of high-quality, synchronized multi-camera video data with diverse poses makes training such models challenging.
results: Generates content-consistent videos across diverse viewpoints with strong inter-view synchronization, outperforming baselines qualitatively.
Achieves significantly higher quantitative metrics for view synchronization (e.g., 527.1K Mat. Pix. vs. 116.8K for I2V-Ours) and superior camera control accuracy (0.12 RotErr, 0.58 TransErr).
Ablation studies confirm the critical roles of the hybrid training data and progressive training strategy in achieving robust performance and generalization.
Successfully extends to novel view video synthesis, re-rendering input videos from new viewpoints with strong consistency to the reference.
solution: A plug-and-play Multi-View Synchronization (MVS) module is integrated into a pre-trained text-to-video diffusion model, using cross-view self-attention to aggregate features across different camera perspectives.
A novel hybrid training dataset is constructed, combining synthetic multi-view videos, multi-view images derived from single-camera videos, and general single-view videos.
A progressive training strategy is employed, gradually increasing the angular differences between camera viewpoints to improve pose adherence and generalization.
paper_summary.summary: SYNCAMMASTER introduces a method for generating multiple, synchronized videos of the same dynamic scene from diverse, arbitrary viewpoints, conditioned solely on a text prompt and desired camera poses. The model quantitatively achieves higher cross-view consistency and camera control accuracy compared to baselines, while maintaining visual quality.

title: SynCamMaster: Synchronizing Multi-Camera Video Generation from Diverse Viewpoints

abstract: Emerging video diffusion models achieve high visual fidelity but fundamentally couple scene dynamics with camera motion, limiting their ability to provide precise spatial and temporal control. We introduce a 4D-controllable video diffusion framework that explicitly decouples scene dynamics from camera pose, enabling fine-grained manipulation of both scene dynamics and camera viewpoint. Our framework takes continuous world-time sequences and camera trajectories as conditioning inputs, injecting them into the video diffusion model through a 4D positional encoding in the attention layer and adaptive normalizations for feature modulation. To train this model, we curate a unique dataset in which temporal and camera variations are independently parameterized; this dataset will be made public. Experiments show that our model achieves robust real-world 4D control across diverse timing patterns and camera trajectories, while preserving high generation quality and outperforming prior work in controllability. See our website for video results: this https URL

keyInsights: Injecting continuous temporal information directly into the attention mechanism (Time-RoPE) and modulating features (Time-AdaLN) allows for fine-grained world-time control within video diffusion models.
A unified 4D conditioning approach, combining spatio-temporal positional encodings and adaptive normalizations, effectively disentangles camera pose and world time.
Training on a purpose-built synthetic dataset that independently varies camera and temporal dynamics is crucial for learning robust, decoupled 4D control that generalizes to real-world scenarios.
originalProblem: Existing high-fidelity video diffusion models conflate "world time" (scene dynamics) with "camera pose" (viewpoint), limiting independent control.
Prior methods for controlling video generation often provide coarse temporal adjustments or rely on two-stage pipelines, leading to inconsistent 4D content.
The lack of disentangled spatio-temporal control hinders advanced applications like true 'bullet time' effects or interactive virtual experiences.
results: BulletTime achieved a pixel-level accuracy of 24.57 PSNR, 0.6905 SSIM, and 0.1265 LPIPS on synthetic data, outperforming adapted camera-controlled baselines by a notable margin.

On real-world videos, the model demonstrated highly precise camera control with the lowest rotation (1.47 RotErr) and translation (1.32 TransErr) errors, while also yielding superior temporal stability and consistency (lower FVD of 2292).

The framework successfully generated complex 'bullet time' effects and other scenarios requiring independent camera movement and continuous time manipulation, showing strong generalization capabilities beyond its human-centric synthetic training data.
solution: The framework fine-tunes a Diffusion Transformer (DiT), integrating continuous world-time control via Time-aware Positional Encoding (Time-RoPE) and Time-conditioned Adaptive Layer Normalization (Time-AdaLN).
It extends this control to 4D by incorporating camera viewpoint conditioning through a unified 4D Positional Encoding (4D-RoPE) and Camera-conditioned Adaptive Normalization (Camera-AdaLN).
A custom synthetic dataset was curated using PointOdyssey, designed to explicitly decouple temporal and camera variations, enabling the model to learn disentangled 4D control.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from ETH Zurich, Stanford University, and CUHK developed BulletTime, a 4D-controllable video diffusion framework that explicitly decouples continuous world time from camera pose. The framework achieves superior pixel-level accuracy with 24.57 PSNR on synthetic data and demonstrates robust, accurate camera control in real-world settings with 1.47 rotation error, enabling novel cinematic effects like dynamic 'bullet time' sequences.

title: BulletTime: Decoupled Control of Time and Camera Pose for Video Generation

abstract: Text-driven video generation has democratized film creation, but camera control in cinematic multi-shot scenarios remains a significant block. Implicit textual prompts lack precision, while explicit trajectory conditioning imposes prohibitive manual overhead and often triggers execution failures in current models. To overcome this bottleneck, we propose a data-centric paradigm shift, positing that aligned (Caption, Trajectory, Video) triplets form an inherent joint distribution that can connect automated plotting and precise execution. Guided by this insight, we present ShotVerse, a "Plan-then-Control" framework that decouples generation into two collaborative agents: a VLM (Vision-Language Model)-based Planner that leverages spatial priors to obtain cinematic, globally aligned trajectories from text, and a Controller that renders these trajectories into multi-shot video content via a camera adapter. Central to our approach is the construction of a data foundation: we design an automated multi-shot camera calibration pipeline aligns disjoint single-shot trajectories into a unified global coordinate system. This facilitates the curation of ShotVerse-Bench, a high-fidelity cinematic dataset with a three-track evaluation protocol that serves as the bedrock for our framework. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ShotVerse effectively bridges the gap between unreliable textual control and labor-intensive manual plotting, achieving superior cinematic aesthetics and generating multi-shot videos that are both camera-accurate and cross-shot consistent.

keyInsights: Decoupling multi-shot camera control into distinct planning (text-to-trajectory) and controlling (trajectory-to-video) phases improves stability and precision for complex cinematic tasks.

A data-centric approach, emphasizing aligned (Caption, Trajectory, Video) triplets and a novel multi-shot camera calibration pipeline, is crucial for learning global spatial coherence in cinematic video generation.
Leveraging pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) within the Planner enables effective inference of complex multi-shot narratives and generation of accurate trajectories from text.
originalProblem: Existing text-driven video generation methods lack precise and consistent cinematic camera control, often relying on imprecise implicit textual prompts or labor-intensive manual plotting.
Current models struggle to execute complex multi-shot cinematic trajectories, treating them as out-of-distribution conditions, leading to generation failures or quality degradation.
A scarcity of high-fidelity multi-shot camera trajectory datasets with global spatial coherence and unified annotations hinders the development of robust cinematic AI.
results: The ShotVerse Planner achieved an F1-Score of 0.422 and a CLaTr-CLIP score of 35.016, outperforming baseline trajectory generation methods on ShotVerse-Bench.
The Controller demonstrated superior execution fidelity with a Translation Error of 0.0163, a Rotation Error of 0.73, and the highest Coordinate Alignment Score (0.500) for cross-shot consistency.

End-to-end, ShotVerse achieved the lowest FVD (281.71), highest Aesthetic Quality (5.465), and highest Shot Transition Accuracy (0.933), surpassing commercial and open-source models in both objective metrics and human evaluations for cinematic quality.
solution: Developed ShotVerse, a "Plan-then-Control" framework that decouples camera control into a VLM-based Planner for generating globally aligned trajectories and a Controller for rendering these into multi-shot videos.
Created ShotVerse-Bench, a dataset of 20,500 clips from professional cinema with hierarchical captions and unified multi-shot trajectories, enabled by an automated camera calibration pipeline.

The Planner uses a fine-tuned VLM to process hierarchical prompts and generate variable-length trajectories, while the Controller adapts a video foundation model with a camera encoder and 4D Rotary Positional Embedding for precise execution.
paper_summary.summary: The ShotVerse framework introduces a "Plan-then-Control" approach for text-driven multi-shot video creation, enabling precise cinematic camera control. It achieved state-of-the-art performance, with an F1-Score of 0.422 for trajectory planning, a Translation Error of 0.0163 for execution fidelity, and an FVD of 281.71 for end-to-end video quality, also introducing a new high-fidelity multi-shot cinematic dataset.

title: ShotVerse: Advancing Cinematic Camera Control for Text-Driven Multi-Shot Video Creation

abstract: We present SpaceTimePilot, a video diffusion model that disentangles space and time for controllable generative rendering. Given a monocular video, SpaceTimePilot can independently alter the camera viewpoint and the motion sequence within the generative process, re-rendering the scene for continuous and arbitrary exploration across space and time. To achieve this, we introduce an effective animation time-embedding mechanism in the diffusion process, allowing explicit control of the output video's motion sequence with respect to that of the source video. As no datasets provide paired videos of the same dynamic scene with continuous temporal variations, we propose a simple yet effective temporal-warping training scheme that repurposes existing multi-view datasets to mimic temporal differences. This strategy effectively supervises the model to learn temporal control and achieve robust space-time disentanglement. To further enhance the precision of dual control, we introduce two additional components: an improved camera-conditioning mechanism that allows altering the camera from the first frame, and CamxTime, the first synthetic space-and-time full-coverage rendering dataset that provides fully free space-time video trajectories within a scene. Joint training on the temporal-warping scheme and the CamxTime dataset yields more precise temporal control. We evaluate SpaceTimePilot on both real-world and synthetic data, demonstrating clear space-time disentanglement and strong results compared to prior work. Project page: this https URL Code: this https URL

keyInsights: Explicitly disentangling animation time and source-aware camera control signals is crucial for robust, independent space-time manipulation, avoiding the limitations of standard frame indices.
Data augmentation via temporal warping on existing multi-view datasets is effective for teaching non-monotonic temporal control to video diffusion models.
A synthetic dataset with dense spatiotemporal coverage is critical for learning fine-grained, continuous 4D generative control and achieving robust disentanglement.
originalProblem: Existing generative video models lack joint, continuous, and disentangled control over both camera viewpoint (space) and scene motion (time).
Dynamic scene novel view synthesis methods are often computationally expensive, prone to artifacts, or limited by strictly monotonic temporal progression.
A scarcity of public datasets exists for training models to understand and generate dynamic scenes with continuous temporal and spatial variations.
results: Successfully achieves disentangled space and time control, allowing synthesis of novel views with independently altered motion sequences (e.g., slow motion, reverse, bullet time) while maintaining scene fidelity.
Demonstrates superior temporal control, achieving substantially higher PSNR and SSIM, and lower LPIPS across various temporal control categories on the Cam×Time test split compared to baselines.

Significantly improves camera control accuracy, exhibiting superior RelRot, RelTrans, AbsRot, and AbsTrans metrics on real-world datasets, and supporting arbitrary starting camera poses for generation.
solution: A latent video diffusion model is developed, incorporating novel conditioning for animation time and source-aware camera poses for explicit disentanglement.
A temporal warping augmentation scheme is applied during training to repurpose existing multi-view datasets and expose the model to diverse non-monotonic temporal behaviors.
A new synthetic 'Cam×Time' dataset is created in Blender, systematically sampling a dense grid of camera viewpoints and temporal states to provide exhaustive supervision for 4D control.
paper_summary.summary: SpaceTimePilot, developed by researchers from the University of Cambridge and Adobe Research, introduces a video diffusion model capable of independently controlling camera viewpoint and scene motion in dynamic videos. The system re-renders a single monocular video to synthesize novel views with effects like slow motion or reverse playback, outperforming baselines in both temporal and camera control metrics.

title: SpaceTimePilot: Generative Rendering of Dynamic Scenes Across Space and Time

abstract: Cinematic video production requires control over scene-subject composition and camera movement, but live-action shooting remains costly due to the need for constructing physical sets. To address this, we introduce the task of cinematic video generation with decoupled scene context: given multiple images of a static environment, the goal is to synthesize high-quality videos featuring dynamic subject while preserving the underlying scene consistency and following a user-specified camera trajectory. We present CineScene, a framework that leverages implicit 3D-aware scene representation for cinematic video generation. Our key innovation is a novel context conditioning mechanism that injects 3D-aware features in an implicit way: By encoding scene images into visual representations through VGGT, CineScene injects spatial priors into a pretrained text-to-video generation model by additional context concatenation, enabling camera-controlled video synthesis with consistent scenes and dynamic subjects. To further enhance the model's robustness, we introduce a simple yet effective random-shuffling strategy for the input scene images during training. To address the lack of training data, we construct a scene-decoupled dataset with Unreal Engine 5, containing paired videos of scenes with and without dynamic subjects, panoramic images representing the underlying static scene, along with their camera trajectories. Experiments show that CineScene achieves state-of-the-art performance in scene-consistent cinematic video generation, handling large camera movements and demonstrating generalization across diverse environments.

keyInsights: Implicit 3D scene representations can effectively guide a generative model when integrated through context conditioning, successfully decoupling static scene understanding from the generation of dynamic foreground elements.

A random-shuffling strategy for input scene context images during training significantly improves model robustness and ensures genuine spatial understanding, preventing over-reliance on fixed input order.

The combination of implicit 3D scene understanding and explicit user-controlled camera trajectories enables precise cinematic control while maintaining high scene consistency and allowing for dynamic subject generation.
originalProblem: Traditional cinematic video production is resource-intensive, requiring extensive physical sets and precise camera control, which limits flexibility and increases costs.

Existing video generation AI models either struggle with maintaining scene consistency across large viewpoint changes or fail to robustly integrate dynamic subjects into static environments due to lack of spatial understanding.
Methods relying on explicit 3D reconstruction are often complex, computationally heavy, and prone to inaccuracies that degrade output quality when generating dynamic content.
results: CINESCENE consistently outperforms baseline methods in quantitative metrics, achieving superior scene consistency and camera accuracy, even with significant camera movements and dynamic foreground subjects.
The model robustly generates vivid and natural dynamic content, avoiding the artifacts observed in loss-guided implicit 3D methods which inadvertently penalize dynamic elements.

The framework demonstrates promising out-of-domain generalization capabilities to real-world scenes, indicating its potential for practical applications in virtual stage production and cinematic storytelling.
solution: The framework extracts an implicit 3D-aware scene representation from static scene images using a Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer (VGGT).

It integrates this implicit 3D information into a pretrained text-to-video diffusion model via a novel context conditioning mechanism, concatenating scene image and implicit 3D tokens with noisy video latents to disentangle static context from dynamic generation.
A custom 'Scene-Decoupled Video Dataset' created in Unreal Engine 5, featuring diverse environments, dynamic characters, and precise camera trajectories, was used for robust model training.
paper_summary.summary: CINESCENE introduces a framework for cinematic video generation that leverages implicit 3D scene representations to decouple static environments from dynamic subjects. This approach enables the synthesis of high-quality, scene-consistent videos featuring novel dynamic elements under user-specified camera trajectories, addressing challenges in traditional and AI-driven video production.

title: CineScene: Implicit 3D as Effective Scene Representation for Cinematic Video Generation

abstract: Camera control has been extensively studied in conditioned video generation; however, performing precisely altering the camera trajectories while faithfully preserving the video content remains a challenging task. The mainstream approach to achieving precise camera control is warping a 3D representation according to the target trajectory. However, such methods fail to fully leverage the 3D priors of video diffusion models (VDMs) and often fall into the Inpainting Trap, resulting in subject inconsistency and degraded generation quality. To address this problem, we propose DepthDirector, a video re-rendering framework with precise camera controllability. By leveraging the depth video from explicit 3D representation as camera-control guidance, our method can faithfully reproduce the dynamic scene of an input video under novel camera trajectories. Specifically, we design a View-Content Dual-Stream Condition mechanism that injects both the source video and the warped depth sequence rendered under the target viewpoint into the pretrained video generation model. This geometric guidance signal enables VDMs to comprehend camera movements and leverage their 3D understanding capabilities, thereby facilitating precise camera control and consistent content generation. Next, we introduce a lightweight LoRA-based video diffusion adapter to train our framework, fully preserving the knowledge priors of VDMs. Additionally, we construct a large-scale multi-camera synchronized dataset named MultiCam-WarpData using Unreal Engine 5, containing 8K videos across 1K dynamic scenes. Extensive experiments show that DepthDirector outperforms existing methods in both camera controllability and visual quality. Our code and dataset will be publicly available.
title: Beyond Inpainting: Unleash 3D Understanding for Precise Camera-Controlled Video Generation

abstract: This paper presents DualCamCtrl, a novel end-to-end diffusion model for camera-controlled video generation. Recent works have advanced this field by representing camera poses as ray-based conditions, yet they often lack sufficient scene understanding and geometric awareness. DualCamCtrl specifically targets this limitation by introducing a dual-branch framework that mutually generates camera-consistent RGB and depth sequences. To harmonize these two modalities, we further propose the Semantic Guided Mutual Alignment (SIGMA) mechanism, which performs RGB-depth fusion in a semantics-guided and mutually reinforced manner. These designs collectively enable DualCamCtrl to better disentangle appearance and geometry modeling, generating videos that more faithfully adhere to the specified camera trajectories. Additionally, we analyze and reveal the distinct influence of depth and camera poses across denoising stages and further demonstrate that early and late stages play complementary roles in forming global structure and refining local details. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DualCamCtrl achieves more consistent camera-controlled video generation, with over 40\% reduction in camera motion errors compared with prior methods. Our project page: this https URL

keyInsights: Explicit integration of depth information significantly enhances geometric fidelity and consistency in camera-controlled video generation, moving beyond limitations of implicit 3D inference.

A dual-branch architecture, coupled with semantic-guided mutual alignment, effectively disentangles appearance and geometry modeling, minimizing interference while maximizing complementary contributions.
Camera pose conditioning primarily defines global scene structure in early denoising stages, while the depth branch continuously refines local geometric accuracy throughout the denoising process.
originalProblem: Existing camera-controlled video generation models lack explicit scene understanding and geometric awareness, often relying on implicit 3D structure inference.
The absence of explicit geometric cues leads to suboptimal camera motion consistency, errors in following specified camera trajectories, and entanglement of appearance and structure modeling.
Previous methods struggled with generating videos that consistently and faithfully adhere to complex, precise camera movements.
results: Achieved over 40% reduction in rotation and translation errors (RE and TE) on established benchmarks, demonstrating superior adherence to specified camera trajectories.
Demonstrated substantial improvements in video quality and temporal consistency, with the Fréchet Video Distance (FVD) decreasing from 109.2 to 80.38 on RealEstate10K in I2V settings.

Ablation studies confirmed that depth guidance, the two-stage training pipeline, and the SIGMA mechanism with 3D fusion were critical for stable convergence, improved cross-modal consistency, and overall performance.
solution: DualCamCtrl proposes a dual-branch diffusion model that simultaneously generates camera-consistent RGB and depth sequences, processing appearance and geometry in parallel streams.
A SemantIc Guided Mutual Alignment (SIGMA) mechanism is implemented for coherent cross-modal interaction, using RGB features for initial semantic structure and depth for later geometric refinement.
A two-stage training pipeline is utilized, first independently training RGB and depth branches, then activating the SIGMA mechanism and a 3D fusion block for stable and effective learning.
