paper_summary.summary: DualCamCtrl, developed by researchers from HKUST, Fudan University, Shenzhen University, and Knowin, introduces a dual-branch diffusion model that integrates explicit depth information for geometry-aware camera-controlled video generation. This approach reduced camera rotation and translation errors by over 40% and improved video quality (FVD) compared to prior state-of-the-art methods.

title: DualCamCtrl: Dual-Branch Diffusion Model for Geometry-Aware Camera-Controlled Video Generation

abstract:
Human-centric motion control in video generation remains a critical
challenge, particularly when jointly controlling camera movements and human
poses in scenarios like the iconic Grammy Glambot moment. While recent video
diffusion models have made significant progress, existing approaches struggle
with limited motion representations and inadequate integration of camera and
human motion controls. In this work, we present TokenMotion, the first
DiT-based video diffusion framework that enables fine-grained control over
camera motion, human motion, and their joint interaction. We represent camera
trajectories and human poses as spatio-temporal tokens to enable local control
granularity. Our approach introduces a unified modeling framework utilizing a
decouple-and-fuse strategy, bridged by a human-aware dynamic mask that
effectively handles the spatially-and-temporally varying nature of combined
motion signals. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate TokenMotion's
effectiveness across both text-to-video and image-to-video paradigms,
consistently outperforming current state-of-the-art methods in human-centric
motion control tasks. Our work represents a significant advancement in
controllable video generation, with particular relevance for creative
production applications.

keyInsights: Decoupling camera and human motion signals during processing, then selectively fusing them, prevents interference and maintains coherence for complex motion control.
A dynamic masking mechanism, informed by learned parameters and human pose priors, allows for precise spatial application of different motion types.
Representing camera motion via Plücker coordinates and human poses via DWPose keypoints provides granular and accurate control over each motion type.
originalProblem: Precise control over both camera movement and human pose dynamics simultaneously in video generation is challenging for existing methods.
Current approaches typically handle camera or human motion separately, often failing to coordinate both without motion conflicts, quality degradation, or limited granularity.
Integrating complex motion control into Diffusion Transformer (DiT)-based video models, which offer superior scalability, requires specific architectural innovations.
results: Achieves superior quantitative performance over state-of-the-art methods, with higher FID/FVD for visual quality and improved metrics for camera motion accuracy and human pose alignment (lower trajectory deviation, superior PoseErr/DetErr).
Capable of generating videos with precise, complex combined camera and human motion control while maintaining high visual fidelity and temporal consistency.
Demonstrates versatility across diverse human activities and subtle movements, enabling applications in virtual cinematography, motion design, and content creation.
solution: Introduces a token disentanglement architecture that processes camera and human motion as separate spatio-temporal tokens.
Employs a "Decouple-and-Fuse" strategy with parallel encoders and 3D attention blocks for camera and human motion, followed by selective integration.
Utilizes a human-aware dynamic mask, combining learned parameters with human pose priors, to selectively apply motion effects to relevant regions.
paper_summary.summary: TokenMotion, developed by OPPO US AI Center, presents a framework for video generation that provides fine-grained control over both camera movement and human pose dynamics. It achieves this by decoupling and selectively fusing spatio-temporal tokens for camera and human motion, demonstrating improved visual quality and motion fidelity in diverse generation scenarios.

title: TokenMotion: Decoupled Motion Control via Token Disentanglement for  Human-centric Video Generation

abstract: We introduce Reangle-A-Video, a unified framework for generating synchronized multi-view videos from a single input video. Unlike mainstream approaches that train multi-view video diffusion models on large-scale 4D datasets, our method reframes the multi-view video generation task as video-to-videos translation, leveraging publicly available image and video diffusion priors. In essence, Reangle-A-Video operates in two stages. (1) Multi-View Motion Learning: An image-to-video diffusion transformer is synchronously fine-tuned in a self-supervised manner to distill view-invariant motion from a set of warped videos. (2) Multi-View Consistent Image-to-Images Translation: The first frame of the input video is warped and inpainted into various camera perspectives under an inference-time cross-view consistency guidance using DUSt3R, generating multi-view consistent starting images. Extensive experiments on static view transport and dynamic camera control show that Reangle-A-Video surpasses existing methods, establishing a new solution for multi-view video generation. We will publicly release our code and data. Project page: this https URL
title: Reangle-A-Video: 4D Video Generation as Video-to-Video Translation

abstract:
Modern text-to-video synthesis models demonstrate coherent, photorealistic
generation of complex videos from a text description. However, most existing
models lack fine-grained control over camera movement, which is critical for
downstream applications related to content creation, visual effects, and 3D
vision. Recently, new methods demonstrate the ability to generate videos with
controllable camera poses these techniques leverage pre-trained U-Net-based
diffusion models that explicitly disentangle spatial and temporal generation.
Still, no existing approach enables camera control for new, transformer-based
video diffusion models that process spatial and temporal information jointly.
Here, we propose to tame video transformers for 3D camera control using a
ControlNet-like conditioning mechanism that incorporates spatiotemporal camera
embeddings based on Pl\"ucker coordinates. The approach demonstrates
state-of-the-art performance for controllable video generation after
fine-tuning on the RealEstate10K dataset. To the best of our knowledge, our
work is the first to enable camera control for transformer-based video
diffusion models.
title: VD3D: Taming Large Video Diffusion Transformers for 3D Camera Control

abstract: World models based on video generation demonstrate remarkable potential for simulating interactive environments but face persistent difficulties in two key areas: maintaining long-term content consistency when scenes are revisited and enabling precise camera control from user-provided inputs. Existing methods based on explicit 3D reconstruction often compromise flexibility in unbounded scenarios and fine-grained structures. Alternative methods rely directly on previously generated frames without establishing explicit spatial correspondence, thereby constraining controllability and consistency. To address these limitations, we present UCM, a novel framework that unifies long-term memory and precise camera control via a time-aware positional encoding warping mechanism. To reduce computational overhead, we design an efficient dual-stream diffusion transformer for high-fidelity generation. Moreover, we introduce a scalable data curation strategy utilizing point-cloud-based rendering to simulate scene revisiting, facilitating training on over 500K monocular videos. Extensive experiments on real-world and synthetic benchmarks demonstrate that UCM significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in long-term scene consistency, while also achieving precise camera controllability in high-fidelity video generation.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Explicit, token-level spatio-temporal correspondence established through time-aware positional encoding warping effectively unifies camera control and long-term memory in world models.
An efficient dual-stream Diffusion Transformer with block-sparse attention is essential for managing the quadratic complexity of attention mechanisms when integrating extensive memory frames.
Simulating diverse multi-view, long-term revisit scenarios from monocular videos via 3D reconstruction provides a scalable solution to dataset scarcity, enhancing model generalization.
originalProblem: Existing video-generation-based world models struggle to maintain long-term content consistency, leading to visual and geometric incoherence upon revisiting scenes.
Achieving precise, user-guided camera control in video generation models is challenging due to inherent viewpoint diversity and complex 3D modeling from 2D observations.
Prior approaches either sacrifice flexibility and detail for explicit 3D reconstruction or lack robust spatial correspondence with implicit 3D priors.
results: Achieves superior camera control, demonstrated by the lowest Rotational Error of 1.01° and competitive Translation Error of 0.11 across benchmarks, surpassing implicit 3D methods.
Establishes outstanding long-term memory capabilities, yielding the lowest FVD (61.47) and LPIPS (0.09) and highest SSIM (0.77) on cycle trajectory evaluations, ensuring consistent scene revisits.

The efficient dual-stream architecture with block-sparse attention enables practical generation speeds of 2.4 seconds per frame with 20 memory frames, and ablation studies confirm the importance of the data curation strategy for robust performance.
solution: Introduces Time-aware Positional Encoding Warping to establish explicit spatio-temporal correspondence by projecting 3D point clouds from memory frames into target viewpoints and augmenting with temporal indices.

Utilizes an efficient dual-stream Diffusion Transformer architecture with block-sparse attention, processing clean and noisy tokens separately to manage computational complexity when incorporating extensive memory.
Proposes a scalable data curation strategy that leverages monocular videos, performing 3D reconstruction and point-cloud-based rendering from novel viewpoints to simulate long-term scene revisits.
paper_summary.summary: A unified framework, UCM, addresses the challenges of maintaining long-term content consistency and enabling precise camera control within video-generation-based world models. Developed by Tsinghua University, Alibaba's Tongyi Lab, and USTC, UCM achieves a rotational error of 1.01° for camera control and shows leading performance in long-term memory tasks, evidenced by an FVD of 61.47 and SSIM of 0.77 on cycle trajectory benchmarks.

title: UCM: Unifying Camera Control and Memory with Time-aware Positional Encoding Warping for World Models

abstract:
Camera control is crucial for generating expressive and cinematic videos.
Existing methods rely on explicit sequences of camera parameters as control
conditions, which can be cumbersome for users to construct, particularly for
intricate camera movements. To provide a more intuitive camera control method,
we propose CamCloneMaster, a framework that enables users to replicate camera
movements from reference videos without requiring camera parameters or
test-time fine-tuning. CamCloneMaster seamlessly supports reference-based
camera control for both Image-to-Video and Video-to-Video tasks within a
unified framework. Furthermore, we present the Camera Clone Dataset, a
large-scale synthetic dataset designed for camera clone learning, encompassing
diverse scenes, subjects, and camera movements. Extensive experiments and user
studies demonstrate that CamCloneMaster outperforms existing methods in terms
of both camera controllability and visual quality.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Frame concatenation across all transformer layers proves more effective than channel concatenation or ControlNet-like architectures for learning global camera motion properties
Ground truth camera parameters significantly outperform pose estimation methods, validating the need for direct reference-based approaches over parameter estimation pipelines
Selective fine-tuning of spatial-temporal attention layers achieves better camera accuracy and visual quality compared to full model fine-tuning
Synthetic dataset generation enables precise control over camera trajectories and scene pairing that would be prohibitively difficult to capture in real-world settings
originalProblem: Existing camera-controllable video generation methods rely on explicit camera parameters that are difficult for users to specify and often inaccurate when estimated from reference videos
Parameter-free approaches suffer from limited generalization, computational overhead from inversion processes, and vulnerability to unreliable priors
Lack of large-scale datasets with paired videos containing identical camera trajectories needed for training robust camera clone models
Most methods specialize in either Image-to-Video or Video-to-Video tasks rather than providing unified frameworks
results: Achieves state-of-the-art camera accuracy with 1.49 rotation error and 2.37 translation error, outperforming CameraCtrl (2.05, 3.12) and CamI2V (2.17, 3.54)
Demonstrates superior visual quality metrics including highest CLIP Score (31.12) and lowest FVD (171.39) compared to all baselines
User studies show 81-88% preference over baselines for I2V tasks and 78-91% preference for V2V tasks across camera accuracy, video-text consistency, and temporal consistency
Successfully handles complex camera movements like "Boom up + Dolly in" and "Tilt up + Dolly in" that challenge existing methods
solution: Direct reference-based camera control using token concatenation along the frame dimension to inject camera motion and content reference videos into transformer-based latent diffusion model
Unified framework supporting both I2V and V2V generation by replacing content reference with zero latent for image input
Selective fine-tuning of only 3D spatio-temporal attention layers while freezing other parameters to preserve generative capabilities
Construction of Camera Clone Dataset with 391K synthetic videos and 1.155M triple video sets rendered in Unreal Engine 5 with precise camera trajectory control
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from CUHK, Zhejiang University, and Kuaishou Technology develop CamCloneMaster, a transformer-based diffusion framework that enables users to replicate camera movements from reference videos without requiring explicit camera parameters or pose estimation, achieving superior performance over parameter-based methods (CameraCtrl, CamI2V) and training-free approaches (MotionClone) with 1.49 rotation error and 2.37 translation error on camera accuracy metrics while supporting both Image-to-Video and Video-to-Video generation through a unified token concatenation architecture that injects reference camera motion and content videos directly into the latent diffusion process, trained on a custom Camera Clone Dataset of 391K synthetic videos rendered in Unreal Engine 5 across 40 diverse 3D scenes with precisely controlled camera trajectories, demonstrating 81-88% user preference over baselines in subjective evaluations and validating the core motivation that even ground truth camera parameters outperform MegaSaM-estimated parameters by 76-87% in user studies, though the approach incurs increased computational overhead from concatenating reference tokens along the frame dimension.

title: CamCloneMaster: Enabling Reference-based Camera Control for Video  Generation

abstract: We propose ReCamDriving, a purely vision-based, camera-controlled novel-trajectory video generation framework. While repair-based methods fail to restore complex artifacts and LiDAR-based approaches rely on sparse and incomplete cues, ReCamDriving leverages dense and scene-complete 3DGS renderings for explicit geometric guidance, achieving precise camera-controllable generation. To mitigate overfitting to restoration behaviors when conditioned on 3DGS renderings, ReCamDriving adopts a two-stage training paradigm: the first stage uses camera poses for coarse control, while the second stage incorporates 3DGS renderings for fine-grained viewpoint and geometric guidance. Furthermore, we present a 3DGS-based cross-trajectory data curation strategy to eliminate the train-test gap in camera transformation patterns, enabling scalable multi-trajectory supervision from monocular videos. Based on this strategy, we construct the ParaDrive dataset, containing over 110K parallel-trajectory video pairs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ReCamDriving achieves state-of-the-art camera controllability and structural consistency.
title: ReCamDriving: LiDAR-Free Camera-Controlled Novel Trajectory Video Generation

abstract: We introduce FaceCam, a system that generates video under customizable camera trajectories for monocular human portrait video input. Recent camera control approaches based on large video-generation models have shown promising progress but often exhibit geometric distortions and visual artifacts on portrait videos due to scale-ambiguous camera representations or 3D reconstruction errors. To overcome these limitations, we propose a face-tailored scale-aware representation for camera transformations that provides deterministic conditioning without relying on 3D priors. We train a video generation model on both multi-view studio captures and in-the-wild monocular videos, and introduce two camera-control data generation strategies: synthetic camera motion and multi-shot stitching, to exploit stationary training cameras while generalizing to dynamic, continuous camera trajectories at inference time. Experiments on Ava-256 dataset and diverse in-the-wild videos demonstrate that FaceCam achieves superior performance in camera controllability, visual quality, identity and motion preservation.

keyInsights: Using 2D facial landmarks as a scale-aware conditioning signal effectively resolves the inherent scale ambiguity of camera parameters in monocular video generation, enabling precise control without geometric priors.

Sophisticated data augmentation strategies, including synthetic camera motion and multi-shot stitching, can compensate for the lack of real-world dynamic multi-view datasets to train robust camera-controllable generative models.

Building upon powerful latent video diffusion models and fine-tuning with problem-specific conditioning can yield high-fidelity and controllable generation even for complex tasks like identity-preserving portrait video synthesis.
originalProblem: Existing camera control methods for video generation suffer from scale ambiguity in monocular inputs, making precise and intuitive control difficult.
Reconstruction-based approaches for novel view synthesis are prone to geometric errors, leading to noticeable facial distortions and identity drift in portrait videos.
Training robust models for dynamic camera control is hindered by the scarcity of large-scale, high-quality multi-view video datasets with continuous camera motion.
results: FaceCam significantly outperformed existing methods (e.g., ReCamMaster, TrajectoryCrafter) on the Ava-256 dataset, achieving a PSNR of 15.85 (vs. 9.73 and 10.32) and an ArcFace score of 0.8574 (vs. 0.7014 and 0.5220).
On in-the-wild portrait videos, the model demonstrated high camera-motion correctness (97.00%) and superior identity preservation (ArcFace similarity 83.94%) across diverse camera trajectories.

Qualitative results showed FaceCam maintaining high visual quality, preserving human geometry, expressions, and fine details (e.g., hair, hands), and accurately synthesizing co-occurring objects, generalizing well to varied inputs.
solution: FaceCam introduces a scale-aware camera conditioning method that utilizes image-space 2D facial landmarks, circumventing the monocular scale ambiguity of traditional extrinsic camera parameters.
A novel data generation pipeline combines synthetic camera motion, multi-shot stitching from static multi-view datasets, and in-the-wild videos to train the model effectively despite data scarcity.

The system integrates a user-friendly inference pipeline where target camera trajectories are defined by manipulating a proxy 3D head model and extracting 2D landmarks for conditioning a latent video diffusion model based on Wan.
paper_summary.summary: FaceCam, from UC Merced and Adobe Research, introduces a system for generating portrait videos with precise, user-controlled camera trajectories from a single monocular input. It achieves superior visual fidelity and identity preservation by employing a scale-aware conditioning mechanism based on facial landmarks and an extensive data generation pipeline, significantly outperforming baselines with a PSNR of 15.85 and an ArcFace score of 0.8574 on the Ava-256 dataset.

title: FaceCam: Portrait Video Camera Control via Scale-Aware Conditioning

abstract: Video inbetweening creates smooth and natural transitions between two image frames, making it an indispensable tool for video editing and long-form video synthesis. Existing works in this domain are unable to generate large, complex, or intricate motions. In particular, they cannot accommodate the versatility of user intents and generally lack fine control over the details of intermediate frames, leading to misalignment with the creative mind. To fill these gaps, we introduce MultiCOIN, a video inbetweening framework that allows multi-modal controls, including depth transition and layering, motion trajectories, text prompts, and target regions for movement localization, while achieving a balance between flexibility, ease of use, and precision for fine-grained video interpolation. To achieve this, we adopt the Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architecture as our video generative model, due to its proven capability to generate high-quality long videos. To ensure compatibility between DiT and our multi-modal controls, we map all motion controls into a common sparse and user-friendly point-based representation as the video/noise input. Further, to respect the variety of controls which operate at varying levels of granularity and influence, we separate content controls and motion controls into two branches to encode the required features before guiding the denoising process, resulting in two generators, one for motion and the other for content. Finally, we propose a stage-wise training strategy to ensure that our model learns the multi-modal controls smoothly. Extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments demonstrate that multi-modal controls enable a more dynamic, customizable, and contextually accurate visual narrative.
title: MultiCOIN: Multi-Modal COntrollable Video INbetweening

abstract:
Motions in a video primarily consist of camera motion, induced by camera
movement, and object motion, resulting from object movement. Accurate control
of both camera and object motion is essential for video generation. However,
existing works either mainly focus on one type of motion or do not clearly
distinguish between the two, limiting their control capabilities and diversity.
Therefore, this paper presents MotionCtrl, a unified and flexible motion
controller for video generation designed to effectively and independently
control camera and object motion. The architecture and training strategy of
MotionCtrl are carefully devised, taking into account the inherent properties
of camera motion, object motion, and imperfect training data. Compared to
previous methods, MotionCtrl offers three main advantages: 1) It effectively
and independently controls camera motion and object motion, enabling more
fine-grained motion control and facilitating flexible and diverse combinations
of both types of motion. 2) Its motion conditions are determined by camera
poses and trajectories, which are appearance-free and minimally impact the
appearance or shape of objects in generated videos. 3) It is a relatively
generalizable model that can adapt to a wide array of camera poses and
trajectories once trained. Extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments
have been conducted to demonstrate the superiority of MotionCtrl over existing
methods. Project Page: https://wzhouxiff.github.io/projects/MotionCtrl/

keyInsights: Explicitly disentangling camera and object motion via dedicated modules within a unified framework enables superior and fine-grained control over video generation.

Integrating global camera motion control with temporal transformers and local object motion control with spatial convolutional layers provides an effective architectural design for distinct motion types.

A multi-step training strategy, leveraging synthesized captions and trajectories, effectively addresses data scarcity by first learning camera motion and then object motion using a dense-to-sparse trajectory approach.
originalProblem: Existing controllable video generation methods often fail to disentangle camera motion from object motion, limiting fine-grained control.
Many prior approaches require retraining or fine-tuning for each new motion template, hindering flexibility and generalizability.
Some motion conditions used in previous methods are appearance-dependent, leading to unnatural object appearances in generated videos.
results: MotionCtrl achieves superior control efficacy, with a CamMC score of 0.0289 for basic camera poses (versus AnimateDiff's 0.0548) and an ObjMC score of 28.877 for object motion (versus VideoComposer's 36.8351).
The framework maintains high video quality and text alignment, recording better FID (124.09), FVD (852.15), and CLIPSIM (0.2319) scores compared to baseline models.

Ablation studies confirm that integrating CMCM with temporal transformers and training OMCM with a dense-then-sparse trajectory strategy are optimal, validating the architectural and training design choices.
solution: MotionCtrl extends Latent Video Diffusion Models with a Camera Motion Control Module (CMCM) and an Object Motion Control Module (OMCM) for disentangled control.
CMCM uses camera poses (rotation and translation matrices) and integrates with the LVDM's temporal transformers for global camera motion control.

OMCM uses 2D displacement vectors for object trajectories and integrates with the LVDM's convolutional layers for local object motion control, employing a multi-step training strategy with augmented datasets.
paper_summary.summary: MotionCtrl introduces a framework that achieves unified and flexible control over video generation by independently managing camera and object motion. The system integrates specialized modules into latent video diffusion models, yielding more natural videos with precise motion control and enhanced text-to-video alignment.

title:
MotionCtrl: A Unified and Flexible Motion Controller for Video
  Generation

abstract: While large-scale diffusion models have revolutionized video synthesis, achieving precise control over both multi-subject identity and multi-granularity motion remains a significant challenge. Recent attempts to bridge this gap often suffer from limited motion granularity, control ambiguity, and identity degradation, leading to suboptimal performance on identity preservation and motion control. In this work, we present DreamVideo-Omni, a unified framework enabling harmonious multi-subject customization with omni-motion control via a progressive two-stage training paradigm. In the first stage, we integrate comprehensive control signals for joint training, encompassing subject appearances, global motion, local dynamics, and camera movements. To ensure robust and precise controllability, we introduce a condition-aware 3D rotary positional embedding to coordinate heterogeneous inputs and a hierarchical motion injection strategy to enhance global motion guidance. Furthermore, to resolve multi-subject ambiguity, we introduce group and role embeddings to explicitly anchor motion signals to specific identities, effectively disentangling complex scenes into independent controllable instances. In the second stage, to mitigate identity degradation, we design a latent identity reward feedback learning paradigm by training a latent identity reward model upon a pretrained video diffusion backbone. This provides motion-aware identity rewards in the latent space, prioritizing identity preservation aligned with human preferences. Supported by our curated large-scale dataset and the comprehensive DreamOmni Bench for multi-subject and omni-motion control evaluation, DreamVideo-Omni demonstrates superior performance in generating high-quality videos with precise controllability.

keyInsights: Explicitly binding motion control signals to specific subject identities through Group and Role Embeddings is critical for resolving ambiguity and achieving precise control in multi-subject scenarios.

Reinforcement learning with a latent-space identity reward model (LIRM) offers a computationally efficient and perceptually aligned way to maintain identity fidelity during dynamic motion, outperforming pixel-level consistency checks.

A hierarchical and dense injection strategy for motion signals, combined with specialized positional encodings, effectively integrates diverse and complex control inputs into a unified diffusion model architecture.
originalProblem: Existing video generation methods struggle with the simultaneous preservation of multi-subject identities and the execution of multi-granularity motion control.

There is an inherent trade-off between identity preservation (requiring static pixel consistency) and motion control (requiring dynamic pixel variation), often leading to identity degradation during complex movements.

Current approaches often suffer from limited motion control granularity, relying on singular signals, and ambiguity when attempting to control multiple subjects due to a lack of explicit binding mechanisms.
results: DreamVideo-Omni significantly outperformed the DreamVideo-2 baseline on the new DreamOmni Bench, achieving higher R-CLIP (0.739 vs 0.731), R-DINO (0.499 vs 0.429), mIoU (0.558 vs 0.212), and EPE (9.31 vs 24.05) scores, demonstrating superior identity preservation and motion control.

User studies consistently showed DreamVideo-Omni receiving the highest preference for Subject Fidelity, Motion Consistency, Text Alignment, and Overall Quality across joint customization and motion control tasks.

The multi-task training paradigm of DreamVideo-Omni enabled emergent capabilities, including zero-shot Image-to-Video generation and first-frame-conditioned trajectory control, without explicit fine-tuning for these tasks.
solution: A two-stage progressive training paradigm (Supervised Fine-Tuning and Latent Identity Reinforcement Learning) built on a pre-trained Diffusion Transformer (DiT) integrates diverse control signals for subject appearance, global/local object motion, and camera movement.

Specialized architectural components, including condition-aware 3D Rotary Positional Embedding, hierarchical bounding box injection, and explicit Group and Role Embeddings, are used to handle heterogeneous inputs and disambiguate multi-subject control signals.

A novel Latent Identity Reinforcement Learning (LIReFL) approach, powered by a VDM-based Latent Identity Reward Model (LIRM), provides efficient, motion-aware identity-consistent feedback in latent space to mitigate identity degradation.
paper_summary.summary: The DreamVideo-Omni framework enables high-quality video generation with precise control over multi-subject identities and comprehensive omni-motion, including global object movement, local limb dynamics, and camera shifts. It achieved improved R-DINO and mIoU scores on the new DreamOmni Bench, indicating enhanced identity preservation and motion adherence compared to prior methods.

title: DreamVideo-Omni: Omni-Motion Controlled Multi-Subject Video Customization with Latent Identity Reinforcement Learning

abstract:
We tackle the dual challenges of video understanding and controllable video generation within a unified diffusion framework. Our key insights are two-fold: geometry-only cues (e.g., depth, edges) are insufficient: they specify layout but under-constrain appearance, materials, and illumination, limiting physically meaningful edits such as relighting or material swaps and often causing temporal drift. Enriching the model with additional graphics-based modalities (intrinsics and semantics) provides complementary constraints that both disambiguate understanding and enable precise, predictable control during generation.
However, building a single model that uses many heterogeneous cues introduces two core difficulties. Architecturally, the model must accept any subset of modalities, remain robust to missing inputs, and inject control signals without sacrificing temporal consistency. Data-wise, training demands large-scale, temporally aligned supervision that ties real videos to per-pixel multimodal annotations.
We then propose CtrlVDiff, a unified diffusion model trained with a Hybrid Modality Control Strategy (HMCS) that routes and fuses features from depth, normals, segmentation, edges, and graphics-based intrinsics (albedo, roughness, metallic), and re-renders videos from any chosen subset with strong temporal coherence. To enable this, we build MMVideo, a hybrid real-and-synthetic dataset aligned across modalities and captions. Across understanding and generation benchmarks, CtrlVDiff delivers superior controllability and fidelity, enabling layer-wise edits (relighting, material adjustment, object insertion) and surpassing state-of-the-art baselines while remaining robust when some modalities are unavailable.

keyInsights: Integrating rich, physically-grounded intrinsic modalities significantly enhances fine-grained control and scene understanding in video generation.
A unified diffusion framework can effectively combine video understanding and controllable generation, leveraging synergies between decomposition and synthesis.
Dynamic, modality-agnostic control strategies are essential for robust conditioning with arbitrary input subsets and adaptability to diverse control scenarios.
A hybrid dataset combining synthetic and carefully refined real-world pseudo-labeled data is vital for training comprehensive multimodal video models.
originalProblem: Existing video generation relies on limited geometry-only cues, restricting appearance control and leading to temporal inconsistencies.
A lack of unified frameworks capable of simultaneously performing both video generation and understanding with physically-grounded, fine-grained control.
Scarcity of large-scale, multimodal video datasets with dense, temporally consistent annotations of intrinsic scene properties.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance in video understanding tasks, including zero-shot depth estimation (AbsRel ", 0.105) and accurate normal and segmentation estimations.
Significantly outperformed existing methods in material estimation (albedo, roughness, metallic) on the InteriorVerse benchmark, with quantitative improvements across PSNR, SSIM, and LPIPS.

Demonstrated superior fidelity and temporal consistency in controllable video generation, with highest VBench scores for video reconstruction and effective single-condition generation, particularly with albedo guidance.
Enabled practical advanced video editing applications, including prompt-based relighting, precise material editing, and physically grounded object insertion.
solution: Developed a unified diffusion model built on CogVideoX, processing a comprehensive set of intrinsic modalities through a shared latent space and modality-specific projection heads.
Introduced a Hybrid Modality Control Strategy (HMCS) that dynamically selects subsets of modalities for flexible conditioning, targets, or dropout, enabling robust arbitrary-subset control.
Created MMVideo, a large-scale (350K clips) hybrid real-and-synthetic dataset with diverse multimodal annotations to address data scarcity.
Employed a three-stage training paradigm, including a self-augmentation refinement stage, to enhance performance and bridge the domain gap between synthetic and real data.
paper_summary.summary: CtrlVDiff unifies video understanding and controllable video generation within a single diffusion model by integrating physically-grounded intrinsic modalities and a flexible control strategy. The framework achieves state-of-the-art performance in both tasks, enabling precise video editing applications like relighting and material changes.

title: CtrlVDiff: Controllable Video Generation via Unified Multimodal Video Diffusion

abstract: Controlling the movements of dynamic objects and the camera within generated videos is a meaningful yet challenging task. Due to the lack of datasets with comprehensive 6D pose annotations, existing text-to-video methods can not simultaneously control the motions of both camera and objects in 3D-aware manner, resulting in limited controllability over generated contents. To address this issue and facilitate the research in this field, we introduce a Synthetic Dataset for Free-Form Motion Control (SynFMC). The proposed SynFMC dataset includes diverse object and environment categories and covers various motion patterns according to specific rules, simulating common and complex real-world scenarios. The complete 6D pose information facilitates models learning to disentangle the motion effects from objects and the camera in a video.~To provide precise 3D-aware motion control, we further propose a method trained on SynFMC, Free-Form Motion Control (FMC). FMC can control the 6D poses of objects and camera independently or simultaneously, producing high-fidelity videos. Moreover, it is compatible with various personalized text-to-image (T2I) models for different content styles. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed FMC outperforms previous methods across multiple scenarios.
title: Free-Form Motion Control: Controlling the 6D Poses of Camera and Objects in Video Generation

abstract: Controllable image-to-video (I2V) generation transforms a reference image into a coherent video guided by user-specified control signals. In content creation workflows, precise and simultaneous control over camera motion, object motion, and lighting direction enhances both accuracy and flexibility. However, existing approaches typically treat these control signals separately, largely due to the scarcity of datasets with high-quality joint annotations and mismatched control spaces across modalities. We present VidCRAFT3, a unified and flexible I2V framework that supports both independent and joint control over camera motion, object motion, and lighting direction by integrating three core components. Image2Cloud reconstructs a 3D point cloud from the reference image to enable precise camera motion control. ObjMotionNet encodes sparse object trajectories into multi-scale optical flow features to guide object motion. The Spatial Triple-Attention Transformer integrates lighting direction embeddings via parallel cross-attention. To address the scarcity of jointly annotated data, we curate the VideoLightingDirection (VLD) dataset of synthetic static-scene video clips with per-frame lighting-direction labels, and adopt a three-stage training strategy that enables robust learning without fully joint annotations. Extensive experiments show that VidCRAFT3 outperforms existing methods in control precision and visual coherence. Code and data will be released. Project page: this https URL.
title: VidCRAFT3: Camera, Object, and Lighting Control for Image-to-Video Generation

abstract: We propose PostCam, a framework for novel-view video generation that enables post-capture editing of camera trajectories in dynamic scenes. We find that existing video recapture methods suffer from suboptimal camera motion injection strategies; such suboptimal designs not only limit camera control precision but also result in generated videos that fail to preserve fine visual details from the source video. To achieve more accurate and flexible motion manipulation, PostCam introduces a query-shared cross-attention module. It integrates two distinct forms of control signals: the 6-DoF camera poses and the 2D rendered video frames. By fusing them into a unified representation within a shared feature space, our model can extract underlying motion cues, which enhances both control precision and generation quality. Furthermore, we adopt a two-stage training strategy: the model first learns coarse camera control from pose inputs, and then incorporates visual information to refine motion accuracy and enhance visual fidelity. Experiments on both real-world and synthetic datasets demonstrate that PostCam outperforms state-of-the-art methods by over 20% in camera control precision and view consistency, while achieving the highest video generation quality. Our project webpage is publicly available at: this https URL

organization_info.name: Shanghai InSpatio Intelligent Technology Co., Ltd.

keyInsights: Directly conditioning generative models on abstract numerical camera poses alone leads to weak cross-modal alignment and limited control precision.
Relying solely on visual rendering cues (derived from 3D reconstruction) for camera control is vulnerable to errors in depth estimation, which can propagate as severe visual artifacts.

A shared query mechanism across both abstract poses and visual renders allows for effective distillation of common camera motion cues, simultaneously improving control accuracy and preserving visual detail.
originalProblem: Existing video "recapture" methods struggle to accurately inject camera motion signals, leading to imprecise trajectory following.
Current techniques often lose fine visual details and subject identity from the source video, resulting in blurring or "identity drift" in generated outputs.
Ensuring robust spatiotemporal consistency and plausibly inpainting unobserved regions in dynamic scenes remains a challenge for novel-view video generation.
results: PostCam demonstrated over 20% improvement in camera control precision, achieving lower Rotation Error (0.0501 vs 0.0649) and Translation Error (0.1021 vs 0.1480) on the OpenViD dataset compared to state-of-the-art methods.

The method achieved higher video generation quality, scoring best on VBench metrics for aesthetic quality, image quality, motion smoothness, and subject/background consistency, alongside lower FID (67.20 vs 85.68) and FVD scores.

Qualitative comparisons showed superior preservation of fine visual details, maintenance of subject identity, and robustness in challenging dynamic scenes where other advanced methods failed or produced significant artifacts.
solution: A Query-Shared Cross-Attention module effectively fuses heterogeneous camera conditions, combining 6-DoF camera poses with downsampled rendered proxy videos.
A two-stage training strategy first enables coarse motion learning from poses, then refines motion accuracy and visual fidelity using rendered video guidance.
The system extends a state-of-the-art video diffusion model (Wan2.1) by injecting source video latents to maintain content consistency while selectively training core modules.
paper_summary.summary: PostCam provides a framework for camera-controllable novel-view video generation, enabling post-capture editing of camera trajectories in dynamic scenes by fusing 6-DoF poses and visual renders through a Query-Shared Cross-Attention module. This approach, from Zhejiang University and Shanghai InSpatio, achieves over 20% greater precision in camera control and superior video quality on the OpenViD dataset compared to prior methods.

title: PostCam: Camera-Controllable Novel-View Video Generation with Query-Shared Cross-Attention

abstract: Recent progress in video diffusion models has spurred growing interest in camera-controlled novel-view video generation for dynamic scenes, aiming to provide creators with cinematic camera control capabilities in post-production. A key challenge in camera-controlled video generation is ensuring fidelity to the specified camera pose, while maintaining view consistency and reasoning about occluded geometry from limited observations. To address this, existing methods either train trajectory-conditioned video generation model on trajectory-video pair dataset, or estimate depth from the input video to reproject it along a target trajectory and generate the unprojected regions. Nevertheless, existing methods struggle to generate camera-pose-faithful, high-quality videos for two main reasons: (1) reprojection-based approaches are highly susceptible to errors caused by inaccurate depth estimation; and (2) the limited diversity of camera trajectories in existing datasets restricts learned models. To address these limitations, we present InfCam, a depth-free, camera-controlled video-to-video generation framework with high pose fidelity. The framework integrates two key components: (1) infinite homography warping, which encodes 3D camera rotations directly within the 2D latent space of a video diffusion model. Conditioning on this noise-free rotational information, the residual parallax term is predicted through end-to-end training to achieve high camera-pose fidelity; and (2) a data augmentation pipeline that transforms existing synthetic multiview datasets into sequences with diverse trajectories and focal lengths. Experimental results demonstrate that InfCam outperforms baseline methods in camera-pose accuracy and visual fidelity, generalizing well from synthetic to real-world data. Link to our project page:this https URL

keyInsights: Explicitly encoding 3D camera rotation via depth-free infinite homography warping provides a robust, noise-free conditioning signal that significantly improves pose fidelity by constraining the search space for parallax.
Decomposing the reprojection task into a known rotational warp and a learned residual parallax enables the generative model to implicitly refine 3D geometry and depth through end-to-end training.

Targeted data augmentation, specifically varying initial camera poses and focal lengths, is crucial for mitigating dataset biases and achieving strong generalization across diverse camera trajectories and fields of view.
originalProblem: Existing depth-reprojection methods for camera-controlled video generation suffer from inaccuracies in depth estimation, leading to visual artifacts and limited error correction due to decoupled processes.
Trajectory-conditioned methods are limited by dataset-specific biases, struggling to generalize to diverse camera movements or focal length changes.
Achieving precise and flexible cinematic camera control in post-production for dynamic video scenes remains a significant challenge.
results: InfCam quantitatively surpassed state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a Rotation Error of 3.162 and Translation Error of 0.438 on the WebVid dataset, alongside higher PSNR (22.677) and SSIM (0.718) on the synthetic AugMCV benchmark.

Qualitatively, the system generates videos with natural pose transitions and high visual coherence, effectively handling diverse camera trajectories and varying focal lengths without introducing artifacts.

Ablation studies confirmed that both the infinite homography warping module and the comprehensive data augmentation strategy are essential for the model's robust performance and generalization capabilities, particularly in challenging asynchronous scenarios.
solution: A depth-free conditioning mechanism uses infinite homography to integrate 3D rotational information directly into the 2D latent space of a video diffusion model, simplifying parallax estimation.

A robust data augmentation pipeline, including trajectory and intrinsic augmentation, was developed to mitigate dataset-specific biases and enhance generalization to diverse camera movements and focal lengths.
The framework integrates these innovations into a pre-trained video diffusion model (Wan2.1) by training only new camera encoder and homography-guided self-attention components.
paper_summary.summary: InfCam presents a robust, depth-free framework for camera-controlled video generation that leverages infinite homography warping and a comprehensive data augmentation pipeline to synthesize dynamic scenes. The system achieved a Rotation Error of 3.162 and Translation Error of 0.438 on the WebVid dataset, demonstrating superior camera-pose fidelity and visual quality compared to prior methods.

title: Infinite-Homography as Robust Conditioning for Camera-Controlled Video Generation

abstract:
We propose a unified framework for motion control in video generation that
seamlessly integrates camera movement, object-level translation, and
fine-grained local motion using trajectory-based inputs. In contrast to prior
methods that address these motion types through separate modules or
task-specific designs, our approach offers a cohesive solution by projecting
user-defined trajectories into the latent space of pre-trained image-to-video
generation models via a lightweight motion injector. Users can specify
keypoints and their motion paths to control localized deformations, entire
object motion, virtual camera dynamics, or combinations of these. The injected
trajectory signals guide the generative process to produce temporally
consistent and semantically aligned motion sequences. Our framework
demonstrates superior performance across multiple video motion control tasks,
including stylized motion effects (e.g., motion brushes), dynamic viewpoint
changes, and precise local motion manipulation. Experiments show that our
method provides significantly better controllability and visual quality
compared to prior approaches and commercial solutions, while remaining broadly
compatible with various state-of-the-art video generation backbones. Project
page: this https URL

keyInsights: Diverse motion types, including camera and object movements, can be effectively unified under a single trajectory instruction paradigm by operating within the latent space of diffusion models.
Injecting features extracted from initial image positions and guided by Gaussian distributions along trajectories offers an efficient and compatible mechanism for motion control in DiT architectures.

Tail Dropout Regularization is crucial for robust performance in practical applications, teaching the model that an early trajectory termination does not necessarily imply occlusion or object disappearance.
originalProblem: Existing methods for controllable video generation suffer from fragmented control, requiring separate modules or specialized designs for camera, object, and local motion.
Users face complex and inefficient workflows when trying to coordinate diverse motion types, limiting creative expression and making advanced video creation cumbersome.
A lack of a consistent and intuitive interface for fine-grained, unified control over various motion elements within AI-generated videos.
results: ATI achieves over 55% accuracy (Acc@0.05) in following user-specified trajectories on a test set, with the Seaweed-7B backbone reaching 59.0% and Wan2.1-14B reaching 55.9%.
Qualitatively demonstrates robust and coherent control over combined camera and object motions, successfully generating complex effects like dolly zooms and managing intersecting trajectories.
Maintains computational efficiency, generating a five-second, 480p video in approximately 8 GPU-minutes, indicating its practical viability for controllable video generation.
solution: ATI proposes a unified trajectory-based framework that represents all forms of motion (local, object, camera) as 2D trajectories of user-defined key points.
The system injects these user-specified trajectories into the latent space of pre-trained image-to-video diffusion models using spatially distributed Gaussian feature masks.
Tail Dropout Regularization is implemented during training to prevent the generation of spurious occluders when user-defined trajectories terminate before the end of the video sequence.
paper_summary.summary: This research from ByteDance Intelligent Creation introduces "ATI," a unified framework for controllable video generation, allowing users to guide camera movement, object translation, and local deformations using a single trajectory-based input. The method achieves quantitative tracking accuracy, with over 55% of frames having point distances within 5% of the image diagonal, and ensures visual quality while maintaining efficient generation times.

title: ATI: Any Trajectory Instruction for Controllable Video Generation

abstract: Recent video diffusion models have made remarkable strides in visual quality, yet precise, fine-grained control remains a key bottleneck that limits practical customizability for content creation. For AI video creators, three forms of control are crucial: (i) scene composition, (ii) multi-view consistent subject customization, and (iii) camera-pose or object-motion adjustment. Existing methods typically handle these dimensions in isolation, with limited support for multi-view subject synthesis and identity preservation under arbitrary pose changes. This lack of a unified architecture makes it difficult to support versatile, jointly controllable video. We introduce Tri-Prompting, a unified framework and two-stage training paradigm that integrates scene composition, multi-view subject consistency, and motion control. Our approach leverages a dual-condition motion module driven by 3D tracking points for background scenes and downsampled RGB cues for foreground subjects. To ensure a balance between controllability and visual realism, we further propose an inference ControlNet scale schedule. Tri-Prompting supports novel workflows, including 3D-aware subject insertion into any scenes and manipulation of existing subjects in an image. Experimental results demonstrate that Tri-Prompting significantly outperforms specialized baselines such as Phantom and DaS in multi-view subject identity, 3D consistency, and motion accuracy.

keyInsights: A unified architecture can effectively integrate scene, multi-view consistent subject, and disentangled motion controls, enabling versatile and jointly controllable video generation.
Disentangling background motion via 3D tracking points and foreground subject motion via low-resolution RGB proxies provides robust guidance for complex dynamics and extreme pose changes.
Leveraging multi-view subject references significantly enhances 3D consistency and appearance fidelity across diverse viewpoints, overcoming limitations of single-view approaches.
originalProblem: Existing video diffusion models often address control dimensions (scene, subject, motion) in isolation, lacking a unified framework for versatile content creation.
Disparate motion characteristics of background scenes (6-DoF camera) and foreground subjects (rigid/non-rigid deformations) pose challenges for unified control signals.
Prior subject-driven generation methods rely on single-view references, limiting 3D consistency and multi-view identity preservation under significant pose changes.
results: Achieved comparable PSNR (16.5130 vs 16.4916) and a lower LPIPS (0.2395 vs 0.2725) compared to DaS in motion controllability, demonstrating superior perceptual quality and subject preservation.
Outperformed Phantom across all video quality, multi-view subject similarity, and 3D consistency metrics, showing a 26.5% improvement in 3D consistency (E_align 0.025 vs 0.034).

Enabled novel workflows like 3D-aware subject insertion and in-image object manipulation, demonstrating generalization across diverse styles and subject categories with natural interactions and preserved identity.
solution: A unified video diffusion framework, Tri-Prompting, is proposed with a two-stage training paradigm to integrate control over scene, subject, and motion.
It incorporates multi-view reference images to establish 3D-consistent subject identity and a dual-conditioning motion control module.
The motion module utilizes 3D tracking points for background motion and low-resolution RGB grids for 3D-aware subject motion, composited into a single motion anchor video.
paper_summary.summary: The Tri-Prompting framework introduces a unified video diffusion model that provides fine-grained control over scene composition, multi-view subject identity, and disentangled motion. It leverages a dual-conditioning module for motion and multi-view subject references, improving 3D consistency by 26.5% and achieving superior perceptual quality compared to specialized baselines.

title: Tri-Prompting: Video Diffusion with Unified Control over Scene, Subject, and Motion

abstract: Prior approaches injecting camera control into diffusion models have focused on specific subsets of 4D consistency tasks: novel view synthesis, text-to-video with camera control, image-to-video, amongst others. Therefore, these fragmented approaches are trained on disjoint slices of available 3D/4D data. We introduce OmniView, a unified framework that generalizes across a wide range of 4D consistency tasks. Our method separately represents space, time, and view conditions, enabling flexible combinations of these inputs. For example, OmniView can synthesize novel views from static, dynamic, and multiview inputs, extrapolate trajectories forward and backward in time, and create videos from text or image prompts with full camera control. OmniView is competitive with task-specific models across diverse benchmarks and metrics, improving image quality scores among camera-conditioned diffusion models by up to 33\% in multiview NVS LLFF dataset, 60\% in dynamic NVS Neural 3D Video benchmark, 20\% in static camera control on RE-10K, and reducing camera trajectory errors by 4x in text-conditioned video generation. With strong generalizability in one model, OmniView demonstrates the feasibility of a generalist 4D video model. Project page is available at this https URL
title: OmniView: An All-Seeing Diffusion Model for 3D and 4D View Synthesis

abstract:
We introduce Vid-CamEdit, a novel framework for video camera trajectory
editing, enabling the re-synthesis of monocular videos along user-defined
camera paths. This task is challenging due to its ill-posed nature and the
limited multi-view video data for training. Traditional reconstruction methods
struggle with extreme trajectory changes, and existing generative models for
dynamic novel view synthesis cannot handle in-the-wild videos. Our approach
consists of two steps: estimating temporally consistent geometry, and
generative rendering guided by this geometry. By integrating geometric priors,
the generative model focuses on synthesizing realistic details where the
estimated geometry is uncertain. We eliminate the need for extensive 4D
training data through a factorized fine-tuning framework that separately trains
spatial and temporal components using multi-view image and video data. Our
method outperforms baselines in producing plausible videos from novel camera
trajectories, especially in extreme extrapolation scenarios on real-world
footage.
title: Vid-CamEdit: Video Camera Trajectory Editing with Generative Rendering  from Estimated Geometry

abstract: By generating plausible and smooth transitions between two image frames, video inbetweening is an essential tool for video editing and long video synthesis. Traditional works lack the capability to generate complex large motions. While recent video generation techniques are powerful in creating high-quality results, they often lack fine control over the details of intermediate frames, which can lead to results that do not align with the creative mind. We introduce MotionBridge, a unified video inbetweening framework that allows flexible controls, including trajectory strokes, keyframes, masks, guide pixels, and text. However, learning such multi-modal controls in a unified framework is a challenging task. We thus design two generators to extract the control signal faithfully and encode feature through dual-branch embedders to resolve ambiguities. We further introduce a curriculum training strategy to smoothly learn various controls. Extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments have demonstrated that such multi-modal controls enable a more dynamic, customizable, and contextually accurate visual narrative.
title: MotionBridge: Dynamic Video Inbetweening with Flexible Controls

abstract: Cinematic storytelling is profoundly shaped by the artful manipulation of photographic elements such as depth of field and exposure. These effects are crucial in conveying mood and creating aesthetic appeal. However, controlling these effects in generative video models remains highly challenging, as most existing methods are restricted to camera motion control. In this paper, we propose CineCtrl, the first video cinematic editing framework that provides fine control over professional camera parameters (e.g., bokeh, shutter speed). We introduce a decoupled cross-attention mechanism to disentangle camera motion from photographic inputs, allowing fine-grained, independent control without compromising scene consistency. To overcome the shortage of training data, we develop a comprehensive data generation strategy that leverages simulated photographic effects with a dedicated real-world collection pipeline, enabling the construction of a large-scale dataset for robust model training. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our model generates high-fidelity videos with precisely controlled, user-specified photographic camera effects.

keyInsights: A dedicated, decoupled architectural component (Camera-Decoupled Cross-Attention) is crucial for preventing interference between camera motion and photographic parameter controls, maintaining scene consistency and visual quality.

Training with a mix of synthetic data from physically-based simulations and a carefully filtered real-world video dataset significantly improves model generalization and robust control over diverse photographic effects.

Specific data construction strategies, such as randomizing irrelevant parameters (e.g., bokeh focal plane when blur intensity is zero) for certain training pairs, are essential for learning proper disentanglement and avoiding unintended effects.
originalProblem: Generative video models primarily focus on content generation and camera motion, lacking fine-grained, photographer-level control over artistic photographic parameters.
Existing transformer-based video generators often entangle spatial-temporal motion cues with appearance-level effects, hindering independent manipulation of cinematic factors.
There is a lack of large-scale, well-annotated datasets capturing diverse photographic effects across real-world video domains for training such models.
results: CineCtrl significantly outperforms text-based baselines in photographic effect accuracy, achieving a Bokeh Correlation Coefficient of 0.5504 (vs. 0.3204 for the best baseline) and demonstrating substantial improvements across all controlled effects.
The framework maintains superior video quality and scene consistency, outperforming both text-driven and stitching baselines, which suffer from quality degradation and inaccurate effect rendering.

User studies confirm a strong human preference for CineCtrl's outputs, with preference rates ranging from 80.91% to 97.65% over baselines, validating its ability to produce accurate and aesthetically pleasing cinematic edits.
solution: Extends a pre-trained text-to-video diffusion model (Wan2.1) into a video-to-video (V2V) editing framework to enable precise control over real-world video footage.

Introduces a novel Camera-Decoupled Cross-Attention mechanism to disentangle control signals for camera trajectory and five professional photographic parameters (bokeh, zooming, exposure, color tone).
Developed a comprehensive data generation strategy, combining physically-based simulations with a meticulously curated and filtered real-world video dataset to overcome data scarcity.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Nanyang Technological University, SenseTime Research, and Shanghai AI Laboratory developed CineCtrl, the first generative video cinematic editing framework offering fine-grained control over professional photographic parameters and camera motion for video-to-video editing. This framework achieves superior photographic effect accuracy with a Bokeh Correlation Coefficient of 0.5504 and maintains scene consistency, demonstrating significant qualitative and quantitative improvements over existing baselines, with user study preferences reaching up to 97.65%.

title: Generative Photographic Control for Scene-Consistent Video Cinematic Editing

abstract: We present Split-then-Merge (StM), a novel framework designed to enhance control in generative video composition and address its data scarcity problem. Unlike conventional methods relying on annotated datasets or handcrafted rules, StM splits a large corpus of unlabeled videos into dynamic foreground and background layers, then self-composes them to learn how dynamic subjects interact with diverse scenes. This process enables the model to learn the complex compositional dynamics required for realistic video generation. StM introduces a novel transformation-aware training pipeline that utilizes a multi-layer fusion and augmentation to achieve affordance-aware composition, alongside an identity-preservation loss that maintains foreground fidelity during blending. Experiments show StM outperforms SoTA methods in both quantitative benchmarks and in humans/VLLM-based qualitative evaluations. More details are available at our project page: this https URL

keyInsights: An automated pipeline for creating multi-layer video datasets from general unlabeled videos can effectively overcome data scarcity, enabling data-driven compositional learning.
Channel-wise fusion of noisy ground-truth, background, and augmented foreground latents allows the model to make precise, localized compositional decisions critical for realistic integration.

Strategically applying foreground-specific augmentations and an identity-preservation loss prevents the model from learning trivial "copy-paste" shortcuts, forcing it to understand and adapt to scene affordances.
originalProblem: Existing generative video models lack fine-grained, layer-aware control, relying on coarse text or static image inputs.
There is a scarcity of large-scale, multi-layered video datasets with explicit foreground and background annotations for supervised learning.
Current methods (e.g., Image-to-Video models) struggle with preserving foreground motion, understanding scene affordances, or achieving true visual harmonization, often producing
results: StM consistently outperformed state-of-the-art baselines across quantitative metrics, achieving the highest scores for foreground identity (84.82), background identity (92.88), foreground action alignment (1.22), and background motion alignment (16.36).
Human and VLLM evaluators consistently preferred StM over all baselines for FG-BG Harmony and Overall Quality, with win rates often exceeding 85% for identity and motion preservation.

Ablation studies confirmed that both transformation-aware augmentation and the identity-preservation loss are essential for preventing shortcut learning and achieving robust, affordance-aware video composition.
solution: The StM Decomposer automatically splits unlabeled videos into distinct layers: a text caption, a foreground mask, a foreground video layer, and an in-painted background video layer, thereby creating the StM-50K dataset.
The StM Composer, built on a latent diffusion transformer, learns to merge these separated layers back into a coherent video using multi-layer conditional fusion.
Training incorporates transformation-aware augmentation on the foreground and an identity-preservation loss to promote genuine compositional understanding and balance fidelity with harmony.
paper_summary.summary: The "Layer-Aware Video Composition via Split-then-Merge" framework, a collaboration between the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign and Google, introduces a data-driven approach for generative video composition by automatically creating multi-layer datasets from unlabeled videos. It achieved superior preservation of identity and motion compared to baselines in quantitative and qualitative evaluations, effectively integrating dynamic foregrounds into backgrounds.

title: Layer-Aware Video Composition via Split-then-Merge

abstract: Motion controllability is crucial in video synthesis. However, most previous methods are limited to single control types, and combining them often results in logical conflicts. In this paper, we propose a disentangled and unified framework, namely I2VControl, to overcome the logical conflicts. We rethink camera control, object dragging, and motion brush, reformulating all tasks into a consistent representation based on point trajectories, each managed by a dedicated formulation. Accordingly, we propose a spatial partitioning strategy, where each unit is assigned to a concomitant control category, enabling diverse control types to be dynamically orchestrated within a single synthesis pipeline without conflicts. Furthermore, we design an adapter structure that functions as a plug-in for pre-trained models and is agnostic to specific model architectures. We conduct extensive experiments, achieving excellent performance on various control tasks, and our method further facilitates user-driven creative combinations, enhancing innovation and creativity. Project page: this https URL .

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Unifying disparate motion control signals into a single dense point trajectory representation effectively resolves logical conflicts arising from combining different control types.
A spatial partitioning strategy that assigns distinct control categories to specific regions within a scene enables the dynamic and simultaneous integration of multiple motion controls.

A lightweight, trainable adapter network can extend existing pre-trained video generation models with comprehensive motion control capabilities without requiring extensive retraining of the foundational model.
originalProblem: Existing video synthesis methods, particularly with diffusion models, often lack precise control over specific motion dynamics in generated content.
Current motion control techniques are typically designed for isolated control types (e.g., camera, object dragging, or local brush), leading to fragmented creative workflows.

Directly combining these single-purpose controls often results in logical conflicts and inconsistencies, such as ambiguous object positions from different camera views or conflicting local and global motions.
results: I2VControl achieved superior precision in object dragging, with an ObjMC of 17.07 on the Manual Dragging dataset compared to 31.24 for DragAnything, and improved camera control with a RotErr of 0.52 and TransErr of 9.93 on RealEstate10K, outperforming specialized methods.

The framework demonstrated significantly higher motion brush effectiveness, achieving an MSC of 64.59 and IoU of 60.34% on the Object Brush dataset, compared to 6.87 MSC and 15.43% IoU for Motion-I2V.

The system successfully integrates diverse control types, allowing for complex combinations of camera movements, object dragging, and motion brush effects within a single video, and its adapter proved effective across various base models like Seedance and Wanx 2.1.
solution: A unified framework, I2VControl, was developed to simultaneously manage and integrate camera movement, object dragging, and motion brush functionalities without logical conflicts.
Diverse control signals are reformulated into a consistent dense point trajectory representation, which includes trajectory maps, motion strength maps, and spatial partition/category maps.
A spatial partitioning strategy categorizes the 3D scene into 'motion units' (brush-units, drag-units, borderland), allowing different control types to be orchestrated in specific spatial regions.
An adapter network structure, designed as a plug-in for pre-trained diffusion models, processes the combined control signals, making the system agnostic to specific base model architectures.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at ByteDance developed I2VControl, a unified framework that integrates camera movement, object dragging, and motion brush controls for video synthesis from a single image. The system demonstrates improved precision and visual fidelity across various tasks, achieving lower ObjMC for dragging and lower RotErr/TransErr for camera control compared to specialized methods, while enabling complex, conflict-free motion combinations.

title: I2VControl: Disentangled and Unified Video Motion Synthesis Control

abstract: Current video generation models perform well at single-shot synthesis but struggle with multi-shot videos, facing critical challenges in maintaining character and background consistency across shots and flexibly generating videos of arbitrary length and shot count. To address these limitations, we introduce \textbf{FilmWeaver}, a novel framework designed to generate consistent, multi-shot videos of arbitrary length. First, it employs an autoregressive diffusion paradigm to achieve arbitrary-length video generation. To address the challenge of consistency, our key insight is to decouple the problem into inter-shot consistency and intra-shot coherence. We achieve this through a dual-level cache mechanism: a shot memory caches keyframes from preceding shots to maintain character and scene identity, while a temporal memory retains a history of frames from the current shot to ensure smooth, continuous motion. The proposed framework allows for flexible, multi-round user interaction to create multi-shot videos. Furthermore, due to this decoupled design, our method demonstrates high versatility by supporting downstream tasks such as multi-concept injection and video extension. To facilitate the training of our consistency-aware method, we also developed a comprehensive pipeline to construct a high-quality multi-shot video dataset. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that our method surpasses existing approaches on metrics for both consistency and aesthetic quality, opening up new possibilities for creating more consistent, controllable, and narrative-driven video content. Project Page: this https URL

keyInsights: Explicitly decoupling and managing intra-shot coherence and inter-shot consistency through a dual-level cache effectively addresses the trade-off in multi-shot video generation without architectural modifications to the diffusion model.

An autoregressive generation paradigm, conditioned on a fixed-size context from the dual-level cache, enables the creation of videos with arbitrary length and shot count while maintaining computational efficiency.

Strategic data augmentation techniques, including negative sampling for the Shot Cache and asymmetric noise injection, are crucial for preventing visual over-reliance and enhancing the model's adherence to text prompts and overall controllability.
originalProblem: Existing video generation models struggle to maintain consistent character identities, objects, and visual styles across multiple shots within a narrative sequence.
Generating multi-shot videos of arbitrary length and with a flexible number of shots remains challenging for current single-shot or fixed-duration approaches.
There is a scarcity of high-quality, consistently annotated multi-shot video datasets and a lack of robust evaluation methodologies specific to this task.
results: FilmWeaver achieved state-of-the-art visual quality with an Inception Score of 8.57 and superior consistency, demonstrating 74.61% character consistency and 75.12% overall consistency, outperforming previous methods.
Qualitative results showcased the model's ability to maintain distinct character identities and consistent backgrounds across up to 8-shot narratives, alongside seamless dynamic video extension.
Ablation studies confirmed the critical contribution of both the Temporal and Shot Caches, as well as the noise augmentation strategy, to achieving high consistency, quality, and prompt adherence.
solution: A cache-guided autoregressive diffusion framework that employs a dual-level cache system: a Temporal Cache for intra-shot coherence and a Shot Cache for inter-shot consistency.

A two-stage progressive training curriculum, initially focusing on single-shot video generation and then fine-tuning on a mixed curriculum with active Shot Cache, supported by negative sampling and asymmetric noise augmentation.

A comprehensive data curation pipeline that segments raw videos into shots, clusters them into coherent scenes, and uses a large language model (Gemini 2.5 Pro) for consistent group captioning across scenes.
paper_summary.summary: FilmWeaver introduces a cache-guided autoregressive diffusion framework that uses a dual-level cache system to maintain visual consistency across different shots while ensuring coherence within individual shots for multi-shot video generation. This method achieved superior visual quality with an Inception Score of 8.57, higher character consistency at 74.61%, and overall consistency at 75.12% compared to prior state-of-the-art models.

title: FilmWeaver: Weaving Consistent Multi-Shot Videos with Cache-Guided Autoregressive Diffusion

abstract: Multi-view generation with camera pose control and prompt-based customization are both essential elements for achieving controllable generative models. However, existing multi-view generation models do not support customization with geometric consistency, whereas customization models lack explicit viewpoint control, making them challenging to unify. Motivated by these gaps, we introduce a novel task, multi-view customization, which aims to jointly achieve multi-view camera pose control and customization. Due to the scarcity of training data in customization, existing multi-view generation models, which inherently rely on large-scale datasets, struggle to generalize to diverse prompts. To address this, we propose MVCustom, a novel diffusion-based framework explicitly designed to achieve both multi-view consistency and customization fidelity. In the training stage, MVCustom learns the subject's identity and geometry using a feature-field representation, incorporating the text-to-video diffusion backbone enhanced with dense spatio-temporal attention, which leverages temporal coherence for multi-view consistency. In the inference stage, we introduce two novel techniques: depth-aware feature rendering explicitly enforces geometric consistency, and consistent-aware latent completion ensures accurate perspective alignment of the customized subject and surrounding backgrounds. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MVCustom is the only framework that simultaneously achieves faithful multi-view generation and customization.
title: MVCustom: Multi-View Customized Diffusion via Geometric Latent Rendering and Completion
